title: Trump Fired the BLS Commissioner for 'Rigged' Data — His Own First-Term BLS Commissioner Called the Firing 'Totally Groundless'
institution: Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), Department of Labor
status: Complete (commissioner fired and replaced; broader data-integrity concerns ongoing)
what_changed: On August 1, 2025, hours after BLS reported that job growth had slowed to just 73,000 for July -- below expectations -- and revised the two prior months' job gains down sharply by a combined 258,000 (the biggest two-month downward revision since April 2020, at the start of the COVID crisis), Trump fired BLS Commissioner Erika McEntarfer via a Truth Social post, writing: 'I have directed my Team to fire this Biden Political Appointee, IMMEDIATELY... She will be replaced with someone much more competent and qualified.' Trump provided no evidence that McEntarfer, a Biden appointee confirmed to a fixed term in 2024, had manipulated the data, and Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer publicly supported the firing the same day, naming Deputy Commissioner William Wiatrowski as acting director. This was the first time in the Bureau's history a commissioner had been fired, rather than completing or being reappointed past their term. Separately, Trump's own budget proposal for the year had already called for an 8% staff reduction at BLS, and the Urban Institute separately documented a broader pattern across federal statistical agencies of data being removed from government websites or having fields altered in connection with executive orders targeting diversity, equity, and inclusion programming.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Directly ordered the firing via social media, framing the weak jobs numbers themselves as evidence of manipulation. Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer publicly endorsed the move, stating she supported ensuring 'the American People can trust the important and influential data coming from BLS.' Notably, William Beach -- who served as BLS Commissioner during Trump's own first term -- publicly and specifically rejected the firing on the record: 'The totally groundless firing of Dr. Erika McEntarfer, my successor as Commissioner of Labor Statistics at BLS, sets a dangerous precedent and undermines the statistical mission of the Bureau... This escalates the President's unprecedented attacks on the independence and integrity of the federal statistical system. The President seeks to blame someone for unwelcome economic news.'

estimated_impact.summary: Beach, in a subsequent PBS interview, gave a specific technical rebuttal to the manipulation claim rather than a general political objection: he explained the commissioner has no direct access to alter the underlying data collected by career civil servants, and that the July revisions reflected genuinely new information (from state and local education-sector reporting) rather than any political interference -- 'no one disputed that we shouldn't use more information then to correct the initial estimates,' he noted, adding that comparably large revisions occurred during his own tenure as commissioner amid COVID, for unrelated reasons. The Urban Institute warned the firing 'undermines the integrity of the federal statistical agency data ecosystem' broadly, since BLS data (alongside the Census Bureau, Bureau of Economic Analysis, and other statistical agencies) underpins decisions made by governments, businesses, and individuals worth far more than any single jobs report.

estimated_impact.caveat: This entry concerns the firing and the specific, technical dispute over whether commissioner-level political interference in BLS data is even mechanically possible (Beach's rebuttal directly addresses this) -- it is a narrower and more specifically evidenced claim than the broader 'data integrity across federal statistical agencies' concern the Urban Institute raises, which draws on separate incidents (website data removal, DEI-related field changes) not specific to the BLS commissioner firing itself. These are related but distinct concerns and should not be conflated when citing this entry.

confidence_note: Exceptionally strong sourcing specifically because the most substantive rebuttal comes from within Trump's own political circle -- his first-term BLS Commissioner, not an opposition figure -- giving both a credibility advantage and a specific technical explanation (rather than just political disagreement) for why the manipulation claim doesn't hold up mechanically. Core facts (the jobs numbers, the firing, the public statements) are corroborated across CNBC, PBS News, and the Urban Institute with no factual dispute about what happened, only about whether it was justified.

title: RFK Jr. Removes All 17 Members of CDC Vaccine Advisory Committee, Installs Replacements
institution: Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP), Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
status: Complete (new panel seated and has already voted on policy changes)
date_of_action: 2025-06-09 (removal); 2025-06-11 (replacements announced); first new-panel meeting 2025-06-25
what_changed: HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. removed all 17 sitting members of ACIP -- the CDC's expert panel that recommends the vaccine schedule guiding clinical practice and insurance coverage nationwide -- announcing the move via a Wall Street Journal op-ed rather than through the committee's standard process. He cited industry conflicts of interest as justification, calling the prior panel a 'rubber stamp for industry profit-taking agendas.' Two days later, Kennedy named 8 hand-picked replacements, several with documented public records of vaccine skepticism or affiliations with anti-vaccine advocacy organizations (e.g. the National Vaccine Information Center). Committee members are ordinarily not political appointees and typically serve staggered four-year terms specifically to prevent any single administration from controlling a majority of the panel; removing the entire sitting panel let the administration seat a full majority immediately rather than waiting until 2028, as Kennedy himself acknowledged in his op-ed. The reconstituted panel has since voted to remove thimerosal from flu vaccines and end the universal newborn Hepatitis B birth-dose recommendation.

primary_proponent.name: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
primary_proponent.role: Secretary of Health and Human Services
primary_proponent.note: Announced and justified the action personally via a Wall Street Journal op-ed and an HHS press release. Kennedy had specifically pledged to Senate HELP Committee Chairman Bill Cassidy (R-LA), whose vote was needed for confirmation, that he would maintain ACIP 'without changes' -- Cassidy later said the promise concerned ACIP's process, not its membership, but publicly acknowledged concern about the outcome.

estimated_impact.summary: A peer-reviewed study published in JAMA (August 2025) by USC Schaeffer Center researchers directly examined Kennedy's stated justification and found reported financial conflicts of interest on ACIP had been at historic lows for years before the dismissal -- income from vaccine makers specifically had been 'virtually eliminated' among members. This is a documented, published scientific rebuttal of the administration's own stated rationale, not merely an advocacy characterization. Multiple medical societies (American Medical Association, Infectious Diseases Society of America) and former ACIP chairs publicly warned the move undermines evidence-based vaccine policy; one dismissed member told CNN 'this will fundamentally destabilize vaccination in America.'

Characterizations of the new members' qualifications and motives, by contrast, come from advocacy/professional-society statements and should be sourced accordingly, not treated as equally definitive.

This combination (agency's own stated reasoning + independent peer-reviewed rebuttal of that specific reasoning) is about as strong as sourcing gets for this kind of entry.

title: Commerce Department ban on Census Bureau statistical-noise privacy protections
institution: U.S. Census Bureau and Bureau of Economic Analysis (Department of Commerce)
status: Ongoing (order in effect as of June 2026; could be revoked by a future administration before the 2030 census)
date_of_action: 2026-06 (order issued; effects unfolding through 2030 census preparation)
what_changed: The Commerce Department, which oversees the Census Bureau, issued an order banning 'noise infusion' - one of the bureau's core techniques for anonymizing survey and administrative-record data before public release, mandated by federal law (13 U.S.C. §8) to protect respondents' confidentiality. Noise infusion has been used for decades, and more recently through a differential-privacy system built under former chief scientist John Abowd, to make it mathematically much harder to re-identify individuals in small-area statistics. The order leaves the Census Bureau and the Bureau of Economic Analysis only one remaining privacy option: 'coarsening' data (releasing broader, less-detailed categories) or not publishing some statistics at all. Data experts warn this could make data for many rural counties and small communities unpublishable, and that 2030 census redistricting data preparation - already underway - will need to be redesigned from scratch. The order followed, and is intertwined with, a separate 2025 lawsuit by America First Legal (co-founded by White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller) challenging the bureau's differential-privacy system, and came after a broader thinning of the bureau's statistical expertise amid Trump administration workforce cuts. It was issued with no public technical rationale, no advance opportunity for outside statisticians to review or comment, and no response from Commerce to a reporter's request for specific examples of the harm it claims to be fixing.

primary_proponent.name: Howard Lutnick
primary_proponent.role: U.S. Secretary of Commerce, whose department issued the order and oversees the Census Bureau and BEA
primary_proponent.note: Commerce, under Lutnick, published the order banning noise infusion; Commerce spokesperson Kristen Eichamer defended it publicly as necessary to 'maintain public confidence' in the data, while declining to provide examples of noise infusion having undermined data integrity when asked directly by NPR.

estimated_impact.summary: Removing the bureau's primary legally-mandated privacy technique, with no substitute of comparable quality, is expected to shrink or eliminate published statistics for small counties, rural areas, and minority communities, and to force a redesign of 2030 census redistricting-data preparation already underway.

figures.value: 2 (Census Bureau and Bureau of Economic Analysis)
figures.source: Commerce Department order, June 2026, reported by NPR

figures.metric: Remaining privacy-protection option after the ban
figures.value: 1 ('coarsening' - broader, less detailed data categories - or non-publication)

estimated_impact.caveat: Commerce's stated rationale is that 'indiscriminate use of noise infusion...ultimately undermined confidence in the department's products and cast doubt on their integrity,' and that coarsening better 'upholds our duty to safeguard the privacy of those who provide information' - i.e., the administration frames this as a pro-privacy, pro-integrity move, not a cut. Independent data-user advocates (Beth Jarosz, Association of Public Data Users; John Abowd, former Census chief scientist) and an anonymous current bureau employee dispute this, calling the change 'cataclysmic' for data production and noting it removed the normal scientific/public review process. Long-running controversy over the differential-privacy system predates this administration - Alabama Republican officials sued to block it in 2021 - so this is a continuation of a genuine, multi-year methodological dispute, not a one-sided fabrication.

confidence_note: High confidence on the existence, timing, and content of the order and the range of expert reaction, based on NPR's direct interviews with the Commerce Department spokesperson, a former Census Bureau chief scientist, and outside data-user advocates, corroborated by a September 2025 Commerce Inspector General audit and a CBPP policy report documenting broader statistical-agency budget and staffing pressure. The ultimate real-world impact on specific datasets is still unfolding and not yet independently audited.

title: CFPB Ordered to Stop All Work, Nearly Defunded — Federal Judge Called It 'Hanging by a Thread'
institution: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
status: Ongoing litigation (court has ordered continued funding through at least March 2026; underlying dismantling effort not abandoned)
date_of_action: 2025-02-10 (stop-work order issued) through 2025-12-30 (federal judge's 'hanging by a thread' ruling)
what_changed: On February 10, 2025, newly installed Acting Director Russell Vought (who simultaneously serves as OMB Director) ordered all CFPB staff and contractors to stop working entirely and not return to agency offices. DOGE personnel were given access to CFPB's HR, payroll, and enforcement-records systems around the same time; Elon Musk posted 'CFPB RIP' on social media. A federal judge issued a temporary restraining order on February 14, 2025 blocking further firings, data destruction, or funding cuts, but by then CFPB's website had already been taken down and staff locked out of buildings and systems. Vought separately moved to cut off the agency's Federal Reserve funding entirely and canceled roughly $100 million in CFPB contracts. In April 2025, despite the court order, about 1,400 employees who had chosen to remain received layoff notices -- a reduction that would have left only about 200 of the agency's original ~1,700 staff. Court filings from CFPB leadership itself, filed in March 2025, proposed shrinking authorized staff from 1,174 to 556, sparing only the legal division (which defends the agency in the litigation over its own dismantling) from cuts. By December 2025, more than 30% of CFPB's authorized staff had departed. On December 30, 2025, the same federal judge overseeing the case rejected the administration's argument that Vought could lawfully withhold the agency's funding, ordering him to request continued Federal Reserve funding, which he did -- keeping CFPB funded through at least March 2026. In May 2026, CFPB removed roughly 1,700 web pages of consumer advisories, press releases, and testimony predating February 2025 from its public website.

primary_proponent.name: Russell Vought
primary_proponent.role: Acting Director of CFPB (concurrently Director of the Office of Management and Budget)
primary_proponent.note: Vought was one of the architects of Project 2025, which explicitly called for Congress to abolish the CFPB (p. 837 of the document). He has publicly criticized CFPB staff in interviews, telling The Charlie Kirk Show that 'all they want to do is weaponize the tools of financial law against basically small mom-and-pop lenders and other small financial institutions.' This is the same official whose separate impoundment-related actions (NIH funding, broader OMB spending disputes) are covered in other entries across this project's Government Services Redirected and Cabinet-Level Legal Exposure trackers.

estimated_impact.summary: The presiding federal judge's own characterization is unusually direct for a legal ruling: she wrote that the administration was 'actively and unabashedly trying to shut the agency down' through funding cuts after being blocked from doing so through layoffs, and that CFPB 'is hanging by a thread.' The agency's own reporting states it had returned $19.7 billion to consumers since its 2010 creation as of January 2025 -- the scale of consumer-facing enforcement work now substantially curtailed. Former 13-year CFPB attorney Lisa Rosenthal, who resigned after the stop-work order, described the halted work (protecting victims of predatory payday loans, supervising student loan and auto-financing practices) in terms of what it no longer was: 'This work that I had carried out for these decades and that I cared so deeply about -- this was no longer that work.'

estimated_impact.caveat: As with USAID, this raises a specific, recurring structural question in this tracker: CFPB was created by Congress (via Dodd-Frank) and its constitutionality was upheld by the Supreme Court as recently as 2024 -- meaning the core legal dispute is whether the executive branch can functionally dismantle a congressionally-created independent agency through funding and staffing actions alone, without Congress formally eliminating it. This litigation (NTEU v. Vought and the related Baltimore case) remains ongoing and unresolved as of this entry's last update; the December 2025 ruling addressed only the funding question, not the broader dismantlement question.

confidence_note: Very strong sourcing: court filings and judicial rulings are primary legal documents, not characterizations, and are corroborated across NPR, NOTUS, the Economic Policy Institute, and legal-advisory analysis from Arnold & Porter (a law firm tracking the litigation for its own clients, a useful neutral-technical source). Vought's own public statements (the Charlie Kirk Show interview, his Project 2025 authorship) are on the record in his own words.

title: CISA Lost Roughly One-Third of Its Workforce, Including Election Security and Threat-Sharing Capacity
institution: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), Department of Homeland Security
status: Ongoing (agency still without a permanent, Senate-confirmed director as of the most recent reporting)
date_of_action: 2025-01 through 2026-06 (rolling cuts across multiple rounds: buyouts, layoffs, forced reassignments)
what_changed: Since January 2025, CISA -- the nation's primary civilian cyber-defense agency, created in 2018 specifically to coordinate federal response to critical infrastructure attacks -- lost roughly 1,000 employees, about one-third of its workforce, through a combination of buyout offers, early retirements, formal layoffs (reduction-in-force notices), and Management Directed Reassignments transferring CISA staff to ICE, CBP, FEMA, and the Federal Protective Service (refusing a reassignment could be grounds for termination). By June 2026, of CISA's authorized 3,292 positions, only 2,324 were actually filled -- a nearly 1,000-position gap. Five of the agency's ten regional directors were serving only in acting capacity, disrupting the primary points of contact state and local governments rely on for federal cybersecurity assistance. Separately and specifically, DHS Secretary Kristi Noem personally halted federal funding for the Multi-State Information Sharing and Analysis Center (MS-ISAC) in March 2025, eliminating roughly $8.3 million of its remaining 2025 budget -- MS-ISAC provided threat-sharing services to state and local governments. The cuts disproportionately hit CISA's election security and disinformation-monitoring functions, which had drawn Republican criticism as politically biased 'censorship' under the prior administration. The agency has operated without a permanent, Senate-confirmed director since January 2025; Trump's first nominee, Sean Plankey, withdrew after senior senators blocked his confirmation.

primary_proponent.name: Kristi Noem
primary_proponent.role: Secretary of Homeland Security (CISA's parent department)
primary_proponent.note: Personally halted MS-ISAC funding in March 2025, a specific, individually attributable $8.3 million cut. In her confirmation testimony, Noem stated CISA needed to become 'smaller, more nimble and more mission-focused,' setting the policy direction subsequent workforce reductions followed. A DHS spokesperson characterized the agency's prior work on election security and disinformation as 'censorship, branding and electioneering,' framing the cuts as 'getting CISA back on mission.'

estimated_impact.summary: Sen. Mark Warner (D-VA), the senior Democrat with oversight jurisdiction, sent formal letters in June 2026 documenting the cuts and warning current hiring plans (200-300 new job offers) are 'insufficient given the scale of threats facing our nation's cybersecurity and critical infrastructure.' This assessment comes as Volt Typhoon and Salt Typhoon -- named Chinese state-sponsored hacking campaigns -- have specifically targeted the type of critical infrastructure CISA was created to defend. Acting CISA leadership has disputed characterizations of reduced capability, testifying the agency handled over 4,000 cyberattack victims and 30,000+ triaged incidents in 2025 despite the cuts.

estimated_impact.caveat: CISA's own acting leadership disputes that the cuts have reduced operational capability, pointing to 2025 output metrics (incidents triaged, victims assisted) as evidence the agency is 'doing more with less.' This is a genuinely contested empirical question -- output volume from a smaller staff doesn't necessarily mean nothing has degraded (e.g., in depth of engagement, regional relationship continuity, or unaddressed threats never triaged at all), but the tracker should present both the workforce reduction figures and the agency's own counter-claim of maintained capability rather than asserting degraded security as an already-proven fact.

The MS-ISAC defunding is the most cleanly attributable individual action (a specific dollar figure, a specific named official, a specific date). The broader 'has this made the country less safe' question is genuinely disputed between CISA's own leadership and outside oversight (Warner) -- present both sides rather than picking one as settled fact.

title: Congress Rescinded $1.1B From Public Broadcasting; CPB Dissolved After 58 Years
institution: Corporation for Public Broadcasting (CPB)
status: Complete (CPB formally dissolved January 5, 2026)
date_of_action: 2025-04 (board members fired) through 2026-01-05 (CPB board votes to dissolve)
what_changed: In April 2025, Trump fired three members of CPB's board of directors; the fired directors argued in subsequent legal action that the dismissal was governmental overreach targeting an entity whose congressional charter specifically guarantees its independence from political control. In July 2025, Congress passed a $9 billion rescissions package (canceling previously appropriated funds) requested by the White House, including $1.1 billion earmarked for CPB through fiscal year 2027 -- the first time in nearly 60 years Congress had voted to not fund CPB. The House vote was 216-213, with all but two Republicans in favor; in the Senate, only Susan Collins (R-ME) and Lisa Murkowski (R-AK) broke with their party to vote against the package, alongside two House Republicans (Brian Fitzpatrick, Mike Turner). Trump signed the bill and wrote on Truth Social: 'REPUBLICANS HAVE TRIED DOING THIS FOR 40 YEARS, AND FAILED....BUT NO MORE.' CPB announced August 1, 2025 that it would begin winding down, eliminating most staff positions by the September 30 fiscal year-end and retaining a small transition team through January 2026 to handle compliance and music-rights obligations. When the Senate Appropriations Committee subsequently declined to restore any funding for the following budget year, CPB's board voted to formally dissolve the organization entirely on January 5, 2026, rather than continue to exist in a defunded, vulnerable state -- CPB President and CEO Patricia Harrison described this as protecting 'the integrity of the public media system' by closing on the organization's own terms.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Personally pushed the rescission package and has repeatedly called CPB a 'monstrosity,' arguing public broadcasting -- particularly NPR -- displays 'extreme liberal bias.' OMB Director Russell Vought, who requested the rescissions package on the administration's behalf, told reporters afterward the administration would 'likely' send additional rescissions packages, saying 'there is still a great enthusiasm for these rescissions bills, because Congress wants to be a part of voting for these cuts and making them permanent.'

estimated_impact.summary: CPB funded more than 1,500 local radio and television stations nationwide; roughly 70% of its money went directly to 330 PBS and 246 NPR member stations. NPR President and CEO Katherine Maher estimated approximately 96% of all classical music broadcast in the United States airs on public radio stations specifically. The impact fell most heavily on rural and small-market stations with fewer alternative funding sources: specific documented effects include KCTS in Seattle abandoning long-form journalism ambitions, WFAE in Charlotte closing its community engagement facility, and WPSU in State College, Pennsylvania considering shutting down its radio station entirely. Sen. Susan Collins, despite voting to preserve funding, articulated the underlying tension directly: 'I share the frustration with the biased reporting by NPR, and I would support defunding it. Nevertheless, local TV and radio stations continue to provide important coverage' -- citing Maine-specific examples like the emergency alert network and local high school sports coverage.

The April 2025 board-firing legal dispute over CPB's charter-guaranteed independence remains a separate, not fully resolved legal question from the funding rescission itself.

title: CPSC's Three Democratic Commissioners Fired, Battery-Safety Standard Withdrawn, Agency Left in Republican-Only Rump
institution: Consumer Product Safety Commission
status: Complete (firings litigated and now final following the June 29, 2026 Trump v. Slaughter ruling)
date_of_action: 2025-05-08 (firings) through 2026-06-29 (Supreme Court finalization)
what_changed: On May 8, 2025, President Trump fired three of the CPSC's five commissioners -- Alexander Hoehn-Saric, Mary Boyle, and Richard Trumka Jr., all Biden appointees -- citing 'neglect of duty or malfeasance in office' without further detail, hours after the three had voted to block DOGE representatives from entering CPSC headquarters to fire staff employees. The CPSC's founding statute allows removal only for 'neglect of duty or malfeasance in office' and requires no more than three of five commissioners share a political party, structural protections designed to preserve a bipartisan agency that regulates the safety of thousands of consumer products. The fired commissioners sued; a federal judge in Maryland ruled the firings unlawful and ordered reinstatement, and the Fourth Circuit declined to stay that order, but the Supreme Court granted an emergency stay on July 23, 2025, allowing the firings to proceed while the appeal continued. During the roughly five weeks the three commissioners were absent, the two remaining Republican-aligned commissioners -- Acting Chairman Peter Feldman and Douglas Dziak -- withdrew a previously approved (3-2 vote) e-bike and lithium-ion battery safety testing standard from public comment; lithium-ion battery fires from e-bikes and similar devices have been a rising home-fire and fatality concern nationally. The same two commissioners released a CPSC FY2026 budget request proposing to cut the agency's funding and staffing and to transfer CPSC's functions to the Department of Health and Human Services, led by Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. On June 29, 2026, the Supreme Court's ruling in Trump v. Slaughter -- which held FTC commissioners' for-cause removal protections unconstitutional -- prompted CPSC Acting Chairman Feldman to state publicly that the fired commissioners' 'claims should now fail as a matter of law,' effectively finalizing the terminations. As of this writing the CPSC operates with only two commissioners, both Republican-aligned.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Personally ordered the May 8, 2025 firing of the three Democratic CPSC commissioners; the firings were defended before the Supreme Court by Solicitor General D. John Sauer.

estimated_impact.summary: A statutorily bipartisan, five-member consumer-safety commission has operated as a two-member, single-party body for over a year, during which a pending battery-fire safety standard was withdrawn and a budget proposal to shrink and fold the agency into HHS was released.

title: DOE Dismantles Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations, Cancels $3.7B+ in Awarded Projects
institution: Department of Energy (Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations)
status: Complete (office wound down; zero FY2026 funding provided by Congress)
date_of_action: 2025-05-30 (first project terminations announced) through 2026 appropriations (OCED zeroed out)
what_changed: The Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations (OCED) was created in 2021 to manage roughly $27 billion in funding for large-scale clean energy demonstration projects -- hydrogen hubs, direct air capture hubs, long-duration energy storage, and industrial decarbonization -- and had committed over $18 billion to about 100 projects by November 2025. On May 30, 2025, Energy Secretary Chris Wright announced the termination of 24 OCED projects worth more than $3.7 billion in financial assistance, citing lack of economic viability and misalignment with administration priorities. Internal documents reviewed by Latitude Media showed a broader target list of nearly $24 billion in awards for cancellation across six DOE offices. Staffing followed funding: OCED's roughly 250-person staff was cut to a planned temporary staff of 35, a reduction of about 86%. In November 2025, DOE removed OCED entirely from its organizational chart, folding remaining functions into the newly created Office of Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation alongside similarly eliminated offices (Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Grid Deployment Office, Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management). Congress's FY2026 appropriations bill, passed in early 2026, formalized the outcome by providing zero new funding to OCED and directing DOE to report quarterly on the redistribution of the office's remaining obligated and unobligated funds.

primary_proponent.name: Chris Wright
primary_proponent.note: Personally announced the May 30, 2025 termination of the 24 OCED projects and oversaw the November 2025 reorganization that removed OCED from DOE's organizational structure entirely.

estimated_impact.summary: An $18-billion-plus federal clean energy demonstration portfolio lost the office that managed it, with over $3.7 billion in already-awarded projects formally cancelled and total targeted cancellations across DOE clean energy offices approaching $24 billion.

estimated_impact.caveat: DOE's stated rationale, per Secretary Wright, was that the terminated projects lacked economic viability and did not align with the administration's energy priorities, which the department has reoriented toward nuclear, fossil fuels, and critical minerals over renewable and demonstration-stage decarbonization technologies -- a shift DOE frames as 'restoring commonsense to energy policy' and lowering costs. Critics, including the nonprofit Clean Tomorrow and various clean-energy industry voices, argue the cancellations forfeit federal investments specifically designed to de-risk early-stage technology for private capital, citing lost private co-investment (for example, more than $14 billion in private capital tied to the $6 billion Industrial Demonstrations Program alone) and pointing to Project 2025's explicit call to shut OCED down as evidence the outcome was predetermined rather than a case-by-case economic viability review. Some project cancellations and workforce reductions elsewhere in DOE's energy programs (e.g., power marketing administrations) were later partially reversed when operational risks became apparent, suggesting the cuts were not uniformly implemented without pushback internally.

The $24 billion cross-office target-list figure is sourced to internal documents obtained by one outlet (Latitude Media) rather than a public DOE statement, and is presented as a reported figure rather than an officially confirmed total.

title: DOJ Civil Rights Division Lost 70% of Its Attorneys — Voting Section Down From 30 to 3
institution: Civil Rights Division, U.S. Department of Justice
status: Ongoing (DOJ actively soliciting internal transfers to refill the most depleted sections as of August 2025)
date_of_action: 2025-01 (initial departures begin) through 2025-10 (Justice Connection's most recent departure count)
what_changed: Since President Trump's January 2025 inauguration, an estimated 250-260 attorneys -- roughly 70% of the division's approximately 365-person attorney roster -- have resigned, accepted the administration's deferred-resignation offer, or been reassigned elsewhere within DOJ. By October 2025, Justice Connection (a support network founded by former division attorneys) estimated the departure count had grown to nearly 400 people, about 75% of attorneys. The Voting Section specifically -- responsible for enforcing the Voting Rights Act -- fell from an estimated 30 attorneys in January to just 3 by May 2025. The section devoted to ensuring nondiscrimination by recipients of federal funds was left with no attorneys whatsoever, per the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights. Assistant Attorney General for Civil Rights Harmeet Dhillon, confirmed in April 2025, sent new mission statements to each of the division's 11 sections shortly after taking office, redirecting the Voting Section's focus from protecting against discrimination toward pursuing voter fraud, and telling the Educational Opportunities Section that its work required 'the full dedication of this section's resources, attention and energy to the priorities of the president.' Career section-chief managers -- in some cases with decades of institutional tenure spanning multiple administrations of both parties -- were removed from leadership roles and reassigned to processing public records requests or complaint intake, which several described as a deliberate push to induce their resignation. By August 2025, DOJ was actively soliciting permanent internal transfers from other CRT sections to refill the most depleted offices (Voting, Employment Litigation, Educational Opportunities), some of which were themselves already down more than 75% of their attorneys.

primary_proponent.name: Harmeet Dhillon
primary_proponent.role: Assistant Attorney General for Civil Rights
primary_proponent.note: Personally authored and sent the new mission statements redirecting each section's priorities. At a Federalist Society event, she likened the division's work under Democratic administrations to a 'speeding train' and, addressing the departing attorneys directly, said 'we wish them well in their future endeavors and their passions. They need to pursue them elsewhere. That's not going to be happening at the DOJ.'

estimated_impact.summary: Stacey Young, an 18-year Civil Rights Division veteran who resigned in January 2025 and founded Justice Connection to support current and former staff, described the scale of change as unprecedented in the division's near-70-year history: 'Every presidential administration has its own policy priorities, but I don't think there's any precedent for an administration almost completely refocusing the civil rights division's enforcement priorities the way this one has.' Joseph Rich, who led the Voting Section from 1999-2005 under both a Democratic and Republican administration, raised a specific operational concern about the reduced Voting Section potentially bringing voter-fraud suits 'right in the middle of an election.'

title: Department of Education Dismantled via Interagency Transfers, Without Congressional Approval
institution: U.S. Department of Education
status: Ongoing (Supreme Court allowed dismantling to continue pending lower-court challenges; Congress has not abolished the department and continues funding it)
date_of_action: 2025-03 (executive order) through 2026-06 (special education and civil rights transfers, the last core offices remaining)
what_changed: Following a March 2025 Trump executive order directing the department's effective abolition and return of control to the states, Education Secretary Linda McMahon -- following a strategy explicitly drawn from Project 2025, written in part by her now-deputy chief of staff Lindsey Burke -- has signed 14 interagency agreements transferring the department's functions elsewhere, since only Congress can formally eliminate the department itself. K-12, higher education, and workforce training oversight moved to the Department of Labor. The $1.7 trillion student loan portfolio and federal financial aid administration began transferring to Treasury in March 2026. In June 2026, the department's last two core offices -- the Office for Civil Rights (moving to DOJ) and the Office of Special Education and Rehabilitative Services, which administers IDEA protections for students with disabilities (moving to HHS) -- were also transferred, over the objection of disability advocates who participated in months of listening sessions McMahon's own office convened. McMahon sought to lay off roughly 1,400 of the department's 4,200 employees in March 2025 (litigated but largely proceeded); an Education Department Inspector General report found 135 of the department's divisions were left with zero employees after the layoffs, and fewer than 20 retained more than five staff. During the 43-day Oct-Nov 2025 shutdown, 87% of the department's staff were furloughed -- which McMahon later cited as proof the agency was 'nonessential.' The Supreme Court allowed the dismantling to proceed in August 2025 while litigation continues in lower courts.

primary_proponent.name: Linda McMahon
primary_proponent.note: Personally described the dissolution as her 'final mission' and has described the layoffs using corporate-restructuring language ('lean, mean machine,' 'reduction in force,' eliminating 'bureaucratic bloat'). McMahon has stated she is actively lobbying Congress to make the transfers permanent, telling members 'when we have completed some of these transfers, that are working incredibly well, then we will...' (statement as reported, incomplete in sourcing).

estimated_impact.summary: Despite the dismantling effort, Congress has NOT eliminated the department and in fact increased its budget (~$79 billion) in 2026 to roughly the same level as the prior year -- creating a genuine tension between the executive branch's operational hollowing-out of the agency and the legislative branch's continued statutory funding of it. A former decade-plus career employee of the special education office warned the transfer to HHS was misguided since 'HHS does not have that [expertise]. It's medically oriented,' not oriented toward the educational-access framework IDEA requires. The department still distributes roughly $200 billion annually even in its diminished state.

estimated_impact.caveat: This is a genuinely unusual and legally contested action: an executive branch effectively hollowing out an agency Congress has not defunded or abolished, using interagency agreements rather than legislation -- multiple observers (Frederick Hess, American Enterprise Institute) note this specifically creates friction for any future administration, since agreements would need to be affirmatively unwound rather than simply restored by inaction. The Supreme Court's involvement so far is procedural (allowing the process to continue pending litigation), not a ruling on the ultimate legality of dismantling a congressionally-created department without congressional action.

confidence_note: Extremely well-documented and current (reporting through late June 2026) from AP, NPR, Bloomberg, and Washington Examiner, with specific figures from the department's own Inspector General. McMahon's own public statements and the Project 2025 authorship connection are on the record, not inferred. The tension between congressional funding and executive dismantling is a real, verifiable structural fact, not a partisan characterization.

title: EEOC Loses Quorum for Nine Months After Trump Fires Two Democratic Commissioners
institution: Equal Employment Opportunity Commission
status: Resolved as a quorum matter (quorum restored October 7, 2025 with Senate confirmation of a third commissioner); the underlying legal challenge to the firings was voluntarily dismissed July 6, 2026 after the Supreme Court's Trump v. Slaughter ruling foreclosed it
date_of_action: 2025-01-28 (firings) through 2025-10-07 (quorum restored); related litigation resolved 2026-07-06
what_changed: On January 28, 2025, President Trump fired EEOC Commissioners Charlotte Burrows and Jocelyn Samuels, both Democratic appointees, along with General Counsel Karla Gilbride -- in the agency's 60-year history, no commissioner had ever before been removed before the end of their term. Burrows's term was not set to expire until 2028, Samuels's not until 2026. Title VII requires three commissioners for the EEOC to have a quorum; without one, the agency could continue routine case processing but could not initiate formal rulemakings or issue, modify, or revoke formal guidance. The agency operated without a quorum for over eight months. In April 2025, Samuels sued Trump, the EEOC, and Chair Andrea Lucas, arguing her termination before the end of her statutory term was unlawful. Quorum was restored October 7, 2025, when the Senate confirmed Brittany Panuccio as a third commissioner, giving the EEOC a 2-Republican (Lucas, Panuccio), 1-Democrat (Kalpana Kotagal) composition, with Lucas subsequently named permanent Chair on November 5, 2025. With quorum restored, the EEOC gained the votes needed to pursue the policy changes documented elsewhere in this dataset, including the EEO-1 data-collection rescission proposal. Samuels's lawsuit remained pending until the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Trump v. Slaughter on June 29, 2026, substantially expanding presidential removal power over independent-agency officials and overturning the 91-year-old Humphrey's Executor precedent that had previously insulated such agencies from at-will removal. Concluding the ruling foreclosed her claim, Samuels voluntarily dismissed her lawsuit on July 6, 2026.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Personally ordered the firings of Burrows, Samuels, and General Counsel Gilbride on January 28, 2025, an action with no precedent in the EEOC's 60-year history.

estimated_impact.summary: A statutorily independent, bipartisan civil rights enforcement agency handling tens of thousands of discrimination charges a year was left without the quorum required to make policy for more than eight months, and the litigation testing the legality of the underlying firings was ultimately foreclosed not by a ruling on the EEOC's specific facts, but by a Supreme Court decision expanding presidential removal power generally.

estimated_impact.caveat: The administration's position, reflected in DOJ's litigation strategy, is that the President has inherent constitutional authority to remove principal officers of agencies exercising executive power, and the Supreme Court's Trump v. Slaughter ruling vindicated that view as a general matter. Samuels's dismissal of her own suit reflects her own assessment that the ruling foreclosed her specific claim, not an independent court finding that her particular firing was lawful on its individual facts. Quorum has been restored and the EEOC is now fully operational; the lasting impact is less about EEOC's current functioning and more about the removed 8.5-month capacity gap and the broader precedent Trump v. Slaughter sets for every other independent agency in this dataset (cross-reference the NCUA, NLRB/Wilcox, and Federal Reserve/Cook entries, all shaped by the same ruling).

confidence_note: High confidence on the firing dates, quorum-loss duration, and litigation timeline, corroborated across congressional statements, EPI, NELP, Littler, Seyfarth Shaw, Holland & Knight, and multiple wire-service reports on Samuels's dismissal of her suit (AP via ABC News, Washington Post, The Hill, HR Dive). The characterization of Trump v. Slaughter as overturning Humphrey's Executor reflects the consistent framing across legal-analysis sources cited in this dataset's other quorum-loss entries (NCUA, NLRB), not an independent read of the opinion by this project.

title: EEOC Moves to End 60 Years of Mandatory Workplace Demographic Data Collection
institution: Equal Employment Opportunity Commission
status: Ongoing (OMB's regulatory review concluded June 9, 2026; as of this update the rule has still not been published in the Federal Register, though the EEOC's regulatory agenda and a July 17, 2026 Sunshine Act meeting notice indicate publication remains imminent, with a comment period projected to run into September 2026)
date_of_action: Early 2025 (removal of Democratic commissioners clears the path) through 2026-06-09 (OMB review of the rescission proposal concludes); final rule still pending as of the last-verified date
what_changed: Early in his second term, President Trump removed the EEOC's Democratic commissioners, eliminating what would have been a blocking minority against a reorientation of the agency's civil rights enforcement priorities under Chair Andrea Lucas. On May 14, 2026, the EEOC submitted a proposed rule to the White House's Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA) titled 'Rescission of EEO-1, EEO-2, EEO-3, EEO-4, EEO-5, and Reporting Requirement Under Title VII, the ADA, GINA, and the PWFA.' The proposal would eliminate the mandatory annual and biennial workforce demographic data collections that have applied since 1966 to private employers with 100 or more employees, unions, state and local governments, and public school systems, covering employee race, ethnicity, and sex data across job categories. OIRA concluded its review on June 9, 2026, clearing the way for the proposal to be published in the Federal Register for public comment. As of the last-verified date, the EEOC had not opened its EEO-1 filing portal for 2025 data, leaving employers uncertain whether any further demographic data collection will occur before a final rule is issued. The move follows the EEOC's 2025 decision to rescind the option for employers to report non-binary employees on the form, and a separate, still-pending EEOC proposal to rescind a 1979 regulation permitting voluntary affirmative action plans. Re-verified 2026-07-19: the proposed rule had still not been published in the Federal Register as of this update. The EEOC's own regulatory agenda projects publication in July 2026 with a comment period running into September 2026, and the Commission held a Sunshine Act-noticed meeting on July 17, 2026 that included the draft rescission rule -- both signs publication remains imminent, but no notice-and-comment period had formally opened as of this writing.

primary_proponent.name: Andrea Lucas
primary_proponent.note: Publicly warned employers against using EEO-1 demographic data, argued such data has been misused to justify practices she describes as discriminatory against white men and, per NPR reporting, posted a December 2025 video encouraging white men to file discrimination complaints directly with her office; she is the named official driving the rescission proposal.

estimated_impact.summary: The federal government's primary tool for identifying systemic race-, ethnicity-, and sex-based disparities in private-sector employment, in continuous use since 1966 and cited in discrimination settlements worth billions of dollars, is on track for elimination with no comparable federal data source proposed to replace it.

Critics, including former EEOC General Counsel Karla Gilbride and the advocacy group Equal Rights Advocates, argue the data has been essential to identifying systemic discrimination, citing the decades-long Bass Pro Shops hiring-discrimination case as an example, and that its loss would force investigators to rely on far more laborious subpoena processes going forward, saying 'we cannot change what we cannot measure.' This remains a proposed rule, not a final one: it must still be published in the Federal Register, undergo a public comment period, and be finalized by the EEOC, a process expected to take several more months from the last-verified date.

Every procedural date and the proposed rule's exact title are independently corroborated across Morgan Lewis, Saul Ewing, Bloomberg Law, HR Dive, and HR Daily Advisor legal alerts, and NPR's June 2026 reporting includes on-record quotes from both Chair Lucas and former EEOC officials representing both sides of the dispute. title: EPA Eliminated Its Scientific Research Office, Placing the Successor Directly Under the Administrator's Political Control
institution: Office of Research and Development (ORD), Environmental Protection Agency
status: Complete (formally eliminated; Congress notified February 2026 that closure is finalized)
date_of_action: 2025-05 (reorganization first announced) through 2026-02-13 (EPA formally notified Congress of ORD's elimination)
what_changed: EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin announced in May 2025 that the agency's Office of Research and Development -- described by AFGE Council 238 President Justin Chen as 'the heart and brain of the EPA' -- would be eliminated and its scientists redistributed into other EPA program offices. On July 18, 2025, EPA formally announced ORD's elimination and the creation of a replacement, the Office of Applied Science and Environmental Solutions (OASES). Unlike ORD, which operated as its own independent division, OASES was placed directly under the EPA Administrator's office -- a structural change critics say subjects scientific research directly to political oversight rather than insulating it. Notably, 'research' does not appear anywhere in the new office's name, which former ORD senior science adviser Andy Miller said reflects what the reorganization was actually designed to eliminate: 'the kind of work that points out that there are still problems.' More than 300 ORD staff, including senior scientists, had already left via deferred-resignation offers before the formal announcement. As many as 1,155 of ORD's 1,540 positions -- chemists, biologists, toxicologists, and other scientists across 10 facilities nationwide -- were identified as at risk of layoff in documents reviewed by Democratic staff on the House Science Committee. EPA notified Congress on February 13, 2026 that the office's elimination was finalized, roughly nine months after Zeldin first announced the plan; the agency has not disclosed which offices remaining former-ORD employees were transferred to, or when OASES's top leadership positions would be permanently filled.

primary_proponent.name: Lee Zeldin
primary_proponent.note: Personally announced and defended the reorganization, framing the mass layoffs using the government euphemism 'reduction in force' and stating it would ensure the agency could 'better fulfill that mission while being responsible stewards of your hard-earned tax dollars.' An EPA spokesperson separately described the restructuring as reflecting 'an unwavering commitment to gold-standard science' through 'integration of scientific expertise directly into EPA's offices' -- the administration's stated rationale is that embedding scientists in program offices improves rather than diminishes the agency's science capacity, a claim directly disputed by the researchers and former officials quoted below. This is the same EPA Administrator whose actions are covered in several deregulation-tracker entries in this project (endangerment finding rescission, MATS mercury standards, etc.) -- this entry covers the elimination of the scientific research infrastructure underlying those separate regulatory actions, not the regulatory actions themselves.

estimated_impact.summary: Independent scientists outside EPA identified specific, concrete functions at risk: ORD maintained toxicology databases used by state and local governments, industry, and nonprofits to assess chemical risks, and its atmospheric/hydrologic models are used by researchers worldwide to study pollutants like particulate matter and ozone. Allen Burton, an environmental scientist at the University of Michigan, specifically warned the closure could delay or prevent new methods for assessing risks from chemical mixtures (which can differ meaningfully from individual-substance risk, a core ORD research function). Rep. Zoe Lofgren, ranking Democrat on the House Science Committee, called it a 'travesty' with 'generational impacts on Americans' health and safety.'

The agency has stated all laboratory functions previously conducted by ORD will continue, though it has not detailed which offices retain which specific functions or capacity levels.

confidence_note: Very strong, extensively corroborated sourcing across specialist and mainstream outlets (Science/AAAS, C&EN, NPR, PBS News/AP, Harvard's Environmental and Energy Law Program tracker, Columbia's Sabin Center for Climate Change Law) with consistent core figures (1,155 at-risk positions, $750M savings, 3,700+ agency-wide reduction) traceable to EPA's own announcements and congressional document review. The structural critique (OASES under the Administrator's direct office, unlike independent ORD) is a verifiable organizational fact, not an interpretation.

title: FAA Air Traffic Controller Shortage — Chronic Structural Crisis, Acutely Worsened by 2025 Shutdown
institution: Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), Department of Transportation
status: Ongoing, unresolved (FAA released a new workforce plan in 2026; current administrator says full staffing may never be reached under the existing structure)
date_of_action: Structural decline since 2013; acute crisis point during the Oct-Nov 2025 government shutdown
what_changed: IMPORTANT ATTRIBUTION NOTE: unlike most entries in this tracker, this is predominantly a chronic, multi-administration structural problem, not a single action taken by the current administration. GAO's own December 2025 report traces the roots to the 2013 budget sequester (which froze hiring), the 2018-2019 shutdown (35 days, further hiring/training delays), and COVID-19 (FAA Academy training suspended 4 months in 2020, reduced-rate training through 2022). The controller workforce declined about 6% since 2015 even as flight volume rose ~10%; the FAA employed 13,164 controllers at the end of FY2025, and 19 of the 30 largest facilities are staffed below 85% of FAA's own targets. What IS specific to the current administration: the 43-day Oct-Nov 2025 shutdown further disrupted hiring and training pipelines and forced acute flight reductions (see the related, separate entry on the FAA's 10% traffic cut at 40 airports). Current FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford told Congress in December 2025 that towers would 'never' reach full staffing under the agency's existing structure, stating plainly: 'The system is designed to be chronically understaffed.' Separately, a 2025 proposal to build a second controller training academy (to relieve the bottleneck at the single Oklahoma City academy) was blocked in Congress amid cost and oversight objections, despite FAA and safety experts' warnings that one campus cannot produce enough certified controllers quickly enough.

primary_proponent.name: Not a single clearly attributable action -- structural, cross-administration problem
primary_proponent.note: Current FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford is the named official who has publicly acknowledged and described the crisis ('we'll never catch up'), but he is managing an inherited, decades-long shortfall rather than having personally caused it. This entry exists in this tracker because the current administration's 2025 shutdown measurably worsened an already-severe situation, and because the current administration's response (workforce plan, blocked second academy) is itself now part of the ongoing story -- not because the underlying shortage was created by this administration alone.

estimated_impact.summary: Investigations into a deadly January 2025 midair collision near Washington, D.C. identified staffing levels and combined controller positions as contributing factors, per a February 2026 official accident report. Congressional briefing materials link the staffing gap to a rise in reported 'near misses' nationally. NATCA (the controllers' union) states the FAA is about 3,800 controllers short of target, with 41% of certified controllers working 10-hour days, six days a week.

title: FAA Cut Flights 10% at 40 Major Airports After Unpaid Controllers Began Missing Work
institution: Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), Department of Transportation
status: Complete (implemented and later lifted after shutdown ended)
date_of_action: 2025-11-05 (announced); ramped from 4% to 10% between Nov. 7-14, 2025
what_changed: During the 43-day Oct-Nov 2025 government shutdown -- the longest in U.S. history -- air traffic controllers, deemed 'essential' employees required to work without pay, began missing shifts in increasing numbers as the shutdown dragged on, compounding the FAA's already-chronic staffing shortage (see related structural staffing entry). Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford announced a mandatory flight-capacity reduction at 40 'high-volume' airports nationwide, ramping from 4% on Nov. 7 to 10% by Nov. 14, affecting an estimated 3,500-4,000 flights daily. Both officials described the move as unprecedented -- Bedford, with 35 years in aviation, said he 'could not recall a similar situation.' Duffy publicly warned the cuts could escalate to 15-20% if the shutdown continued, and separately said he could 'shut the whole airspace down' if it dragged on further. The order also restricted private jets from high-traffic airports to preserve capacity for commercial aviation. The reduction produced immediate, measurable disruption: over 1,000 flights canceled the first day, more than 2,900 cancellations and 10,000+ delays the following Sunday, and by the second weekend nearly 2,200 cancellations in a single day.

primary_proponent.name: Sean Duffy
primary_proponent.note: Announced and defended the decision personally in multiple press briefings alongside FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford, who made the specific operational call. Duffy explicitly and repeatedly tied the cause to controllers working without pay during the shutdown, not any independent FAA policy decision -- this is a downstream consequence of the broader Oct-Nov 2025 shutdown, not an isolated FAA action.

estimated_impact.summary: This was framed by officials as a safety-preserving measure (reducing controller workload to prevent errors) rather than a punitive action, and aviation safety experts generally agreed with that framing -- a former NTSB/FAA accident investigator called it appropriate risk reduction, while also noting the real economic cost to airlines and 'massive inconvenience' to travelers. The scale of disruption was immediate and large: over 12,000 American Airlines customers alone had flights canceled on the first day of the reduction.

title: FDA vaccine/biologics division leadership purge and COVID vaccine access restriction
institution: FDA — Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (CBER)
status: Ongoing (acting leadership in place; permanent successor search underway as of May 2026)
date_of_action: March 2025 - April 2026
what_changed: CBER, the FDA division that regulates vaccines, gene therapies, and blood products, went through six different directors across it and its sister division (CDER) since January 2025. Longtime CBER director Peter Marks resigned in March 2025 amid reported disagreements with HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. over vaccine policy. Vinay Prasad was installed as CBER director in May 2025. In July 2025 Prasad was forced out after backlash over his handling of Sarepta Therapeutics' Duchenne muscular dystrophy gene therapy, then reinstated roughly two weeks later after Commissioner Marty Makary intervened. Upon returning, Prasad fired the official in charge of vaccine safety/surveillance and took direct control of that function himself, and went on to push out at least seven other agency leaders during his second tenure. In May 2025, Makary and Prasad co-authored a New England Journal of Medicine framework limiting FDA approval of updated COVID-19 vaccines to adults 65+ and people with qualifying risk factors, reversing the prior policy of a universal annual-vaccine recommendation for everyone 6 months and older; manufacturers must now run new randomized trials to get approval for healthy people outside that group. Prasad also repeatedly overruled career staff on individual drug and vaccine decisions (initially refusing to review Moderna's mRNA flu vaccine, reversed only after White House pressure; rejecting or discouraging gene-therapy applications from uniQure, Ultragenyx, Capricor, and Replimune). Prasad departed CBER for a second and final time on April 30, 2026; deputy director Katherine Szarama was elevated to acting director.

primary_proponent.name: Vinay Prasad
primary_proponent.role: Director, FDA Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (May-July 2025, Aug 2025-Apr 2026); also served as FDA Chief Scientific and Medical Officer
primary_proponent.note: Co-authored the NEJM framework restricting COVID vaccine approval to high-risk groups; fired the FDA's vaccine-safety-and-surveillance lead and took over that role himself; personally signed the initial refusal-to-review letter for Moderna's flu vaccine; pushed out at least seven other CBER leaders during his second tenure, according to agency officials cited by STAT.

estimated_impact.summary: Constant leadership churn destabilized the FDA division responsible for vaccine and gene-therapy approvals, while a policy rewrite removed the default annual-vaccine recommendation for the majority of the U.S. population under 65, shifting them to a prescription/out-of-pocket access model.

estimated_impact.caveat: FDA leadership frames these as deliberate, evidence-based reforms rather than harmful disruption: Commissioner Makary credited Prasad with four 'long-lasting' reforms (a 2-to-1 pivotal-trial requirement, national priority review vouchers, the risk-stratified vaccine framework, and a new 'plausible mechanism' pathway for ultra-rare diseases), and said CBER hit a record number of approvals in December 2025. FDA officials also point to comparable age/risk-based vaccine policies in Canada, Denmark, and Australia as precedent. On the other side, 12 former FDA commissioners warned the new vaccine evidence bar could make it commercially unworkable for manufacturers to develop new vaccines, more than 75 medical organizations criticized the policy shift, and multiple biotech companies said the agency reversed prior agreements on trial design after their programs were already underway. A November 2025 internal Prasad memo claiming COVID vaccines caused at least 10 pediatric deaths was reported by the Washington Post but no supporting case data was released, leaving that specific claim unverified and contested.

No formal GAO or Inspector General report on this specific matter has been identified yet; impact figures (drug denial count, population access figures) come from industry analysis (RTW Investments) and the FDA's own framework rather than an independent audit.

title: Trump's Attempt to Fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook Blocked by Supreme Court, Fed Independence Preserved
institution: Federal Reserve Board of Governors
status: Blocked (Supreme Court allowed Cook to remain in office pending further litigation; underlying 'for cause' merits not yet decided)
date_of_action: 2025-08-25 (attempted firing) through 2026-06-29 (Supreme Court ruling)
what_changed: In August 2025, President Trump attempted to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook -- the first attempted removal of a sitting Fed governor in the central bank's 111-year history -- citing mortgage-fraud allegations against her from before she joined the Fed, raised by FHFA Director (and later Director of National Intelligence) William Pulte. Cook, who denied the allegations and was never charged, sued, arguing her removal was not 'for cause' as the Federal Reserve Act requires and that she was denied due process. A federal district court blocked the firing with a preliminary injunction; the D.C. Circuit declined to lift it; and on June 29, 2026 -- the same day it ruled against FTC Commissioner Rebecca Slaughter in the companion case Trump v. Slaughter -- the Supreme Court ruled 5-4 in Trump v. Cook that Cook could remain in her position while her lawsuit proceeds. Chief Justice Roberts, writing for a majority that included conservative Justice Kavanaugh alongside the Court's three liberals, held that accepting the administration's position 'would in effect transform the Federal Reserve's for-cause protection into at-will employment' and stressed that 'not only the fact of independence but also the appearance of independence is key to the Federal Reserve's design.' The Court explicitly carved the Federal Reserve out of the broader independence-narrowing logic it applied to the FTC, CPSC, NCUA, and other agencies the same day. Cook remained in her seat throughout, participating in the Fed's September 2025 rate-setting meeting. Trump responded on Truth Social that the ruling was 'strictly procedural' and vowed further action; Pulte stood by his fraud allegations. Re-verified 2026-07-19: no further procedural movement identified since the June 29, 2026 Supreme Court ruling; the case remains before the lower courts on the underlying 'for cause' merits question, unchanged from the prior update.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Personally announced Cook 'must resign' and then attempted her termination in August 2025, based on allegations forwarded by FHFA Director William Pulte; both the district court and the Supreme Court found the administration had not shown Cook was actually unfit to serve.

estimated_impact.summary: An attempted removal of a sitting Federal Reserve governor -- widely described by Fed Chair Jerome Powell as 'perhaps the most important legal case in the Fed's 113-year history' -- was blocked, preserving the central bank's independence from at-will presidential removal even as the Court simultaneously stripped similar protections from most other independent agencies the same day.

figures.value: 5-4 (Roberts and Kavanaugh joined the three liberal justices)
figures.source: SCOTUSblog, Trump v. Cook opinion

figures.value: approximately 10 months (August 2025 firing attempt to June 2026 ruling)

figures.metric: Fed governors' statutory term length
figures.value: 14 years, staggered, by design to prevent single-president control
figures.source: Federal Reserve Act, cited in CNBC and SCOTUSblog coverage

estimated_impact.caveat: The administration's stated rationale was the mortgage-fraud allegations against Cook, which she denies and for which she has not been charged; Pulte has said he believes she 'will be indicted.' Cook and her attorneys have characterized the allegations as pretextual, arguing the real motive was her refusal to lower interest rates as Trump demanded. The Supreme Court's ruling was explicitly procedural and preliminary -- it found the administration had not shown a likelihood of success on the merits, not that Cook could never be removed; litigation over whether she was fired 'for cause' continues. This is included as a rare reversal/blocked story for balance: unlike the FTC, CPSC, and NCUA entries in this tracker, the Fed's independence held, at least for now.

confidence_note: High confidence. Directly sourced to the Supreme Court's own opinion in Trump v. Cook (25A312, decided June 29, 2026), SCOTUSblog's contemporaneous reporting, and on-record statements from Cook, Trump, and Pulte reported by CNBC, Newsweek, and NBC News. The 'for cause' merits question remains genuinely unresolved and is presented as such. Re-verified 2026-07-19: no material change to report.

title: FEMA Capacity Collapse — Staff Exodus and Spending Bottleneck Delayed Texas Flood Response
institution: Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), Department of Homeland Security
status: Ongoing institutional instability (three different acting administrators within the year; agency's future role still under review)
date_of_action: Staff departures throughout 2025; acute failure documented July 4-10, 2025 during Texas floods
what_changed: Throughout 2025, FEMA underwent a wave of staff departures -- about 18% of the agency's permanent full-time employees had left as of June 2025, including 24 senior-level staffers, per GAO. In May 2025 alone, 16 senior officials departed, taking with them a combined 200+ years of disaster-response expertise. Separately, DHS Secretary Kristi Noem implemented a rule requiring her personal sign-off on any FEMA expenditure over $100,000 -- for an agency whose disaster costs routinely run into the billions, officials described this threshold as effectively 'pennies.' This bottleneck had direct, documented consequences during the deadly July 4, 2025 Texas Hill Country floods: FEMA's disaster survivor call-center contract funding lapsed on July 5, and it took Noem five days -- until July 10 -- to approve renewed funding, during which most survivor calls went unanswered (an internal memo documented the call-answer rate collapsing from 99.9% to 20%, with survivors waiting over 90 minutes). Noem also did not authorize deployment of FEMA's Urban Search and Rescue teams until more than 72 hours after the flooding began. Acting FEMA Administrator David Richardson did not travel to Texas for a full week after the disaster, telling a House committee he had stayed in Washington to 'kick down the doors of bureaucracy' -- and separately acknowledged he had been camping with his sons over the July 4 weekend when the floods hit. Richardson resigned in November 2025 after six months in the role, the second of three different acting administrators to lead FEMA within the year.

primary_proponent.name: Kristi Noem
primary_proponent.role: Secretary of Homeland Security (FEMA's parent department)
primary_proponent.note: Noem personally implemented and enforced the $100,000 spending-approval rule that created the call-center funding bottleneck. A DHS spokesperson publicly defended the change as shifting FEMA from 'bloated, DC-centric dead weight to a lean, deployable disaster force,' attributing the framing directly to Noem's leadership. Acting Administrator David Richardson's own July 10 memo to Noem -- which he signed personally -- directly contradicts a later public FEMA statement claiming the call center 'responded to every caller swiftly and efficiently.'

estimated_impact.summary: This is a case with an unusually direct documentary link between a specific administrative policy (the $100K approval rule) and a specific, measurable service failure (the call-center funding lapse) during an actual deadly disaster -- not a hypothetical or projected harm. NPR's reporting is built on internal FEMA records (the July 10 memo, call-center logs) obtained directly, not secondhand characterization.

title: Forest Service Chief's Own Internal Memo Contradicted Agriculture Secretary's Public 'Fully Staffed' Claims — 5,000 Firefighting Positions Vacant
institution: U.S. Forest Service, Department of Agriculture
status: Ongoing (2025 fire season managed under acknowledged shortfall; further reorganization/consolidation with Interior Dept. underway)
date_of_action: 2025-02 (initial layoffs) through 2025-08 (peak fire-season staffing crisis and reorganization announcement)
what_changed: In February 2025, the Trump administration laid off approximately 700 Forest Service employees who support wildland fire operations -- human resource managers, ecologists, and trail-crew workers who hold 'red card' fire-response certifications even though their primary jobs are not firefighting. Many were subsequently rehired, but the administration then pushed remaining staff toward deferred-resignation and early-retirement offers; ultimately at least 1,800 fire-qualified ('red-carded') employees left the agency, out of 4,800 total Forest Service departures in 2025 (more than 13% of the agency's total workforce). Internal Forest Service data reviewed by ProPublica, High Country News, and Capital & Main found at least 5,000 firefighting positions vacant as of mid-July 2025 -- directly contradicting the agency's own public claim of having exceeded its 2025 hiring goal (11,364 firefighters on board by end of July). USDA spokesperson Cat McRae disputed the vacancy data as reflecting 'outdated org charts and unfunded positions'; ProPublica confirmed its analysis had already excluded unfunded positions. Days after that public dispute, a memo Forest Service Chief Tom Schultz sent to agency leadership -- obtained by High Country News -- stated plainly: 'As expected, the 2025 Fire Year is proving to be extremely challenging... we know the demand for resources outpaces their availability,' and directed that all red-carded employees, including IT and HR staff, be made available for fire assignments. At an internal spring planning meeting, one employee was told directly, 'Help is not on the way' -- a phrase the employee said they had 'never been told before' in their career. The agency subsequently took the unusual step of waiving a statutory 1,039-hour cap on seasonal-employee work hours (raising it to 1,560 hours) and asked 1,400 firefighting-qualified former employees to return; as of August 12, only 100 had accepted. Separately, USDA announced a reorganization eliminating the Forest Service's nine regional offices and consolidating them into five 'hub' locations, with Secretary Brooke Rollins acknowledging up to half of affected staff may not relocate.

primary_proponent.name: Brooke Rollins
primary_proponent.note: Publicly maintained the Forest Service was 'fully equipped and operationally ready to protect people and communities from wildfire' even as her own agency's Chief, Tom Schultz, was privately telling leadership the opposite in writing. A USDA spokesperson later attempted to reconcile the two statements by saying Schultz's memo referred to a broader pool of red-carded employees who could be 'called on to bolster' response as fire activity increases, while Rollins's statement referred only to full-time firefighters -- a real distinction, though one that doesn't fully resolve why the agency disputed the underlying vacancy data as inaccurate while its own Chief was independently describing a resource shortfall in nearly identical terms.

estimated_impact.summary: This is one of the more directly self-contradicting cases in this tracker: the dispute isn't primarily outside advocates versus the administration, but the Agriculture Secretary's public statements versus her own Forest Service Chief's internal, contemporaneous memo -- both documented, both on the record. Frontline accounts corroborate the operational strain described in Schultz's memo: a California Forest Service survey found 26% of engine captain positions and 42% of engineer positions vacant, and wildland firefighters described being assigned non-firefighting duties like trail-building and lawn-mowing due to short-staffing elsewhere in the agency.

title: FTC Cuts Workforce 10%, Loses Bipartisan Independence After Supreme Court Upholds Commissioner Firings
institution: Federal Trade Commission
status: Complete (workforce reduction implemented in 2025; commissioner-firing litigation permanently resolved by the Supreme Court on June 29, 2026)
date_of_action: 2025-03-18 (commissioner firings) through 2026-06-29 (Supreme Court ruling); staff reduction implemented through 2025
what_changed: On March 18, 2025, President Trump fired the FTC's two Democratic commissioners, Rebecca Slaughter and Alvaro Bedoya, without citing any statutory cause, instead stating their continued service was 'inconsistent with my Administration's priorities.' This left the five-seat Commission with only its two sitting Republican members. Slaughter and Bedoya sued; Bedoya later resigned and dropped his claim, but Slaughter's case proceeded, with a district court initially ordering her reinstated. Separately, on May 15, 2025, Chairman Andrew Ferguson testified to Congress that the FTC was implementing a 10% workforce reduction, bringing headcount to approximately 1,100 employees (down from 1,221), the agency's lowest staffing level in a decade. On June 29, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Trump v. Slaughter that the FTC's for-cause removal protections for commissioners are unconstitutional, permanently allowing the president to remove commissioners at will and explicitly overruling the 1935 precedent Humphrey's Executor v. United States, which had protected the independence of the FTC and similar agencies for 90 years. Re-verified 2026-07-19: the Commission remains at two sitting Republican commissioners (Chair Andrew Ferguson and Mark Meador) as of this update, with legal commentary noting little short-term incentive to restore a five-member bipartisan structure -- no material change to the entry's core claims.

title: Fish and Wildlife Service Loses Over 1,500 Staff; Zero New Species Listed as Endangered
institution: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service
status: Ongoing
date_of_action: 2025-01 to 2026-02 (staffing reductions); ongoing effects on listing backlog through the last-verified date
what_changed: Between January 2025 and February 2026, the Fish and Wildlife Service saw a net reduction of 1,538 employees, according to Office of Personnel Management data compiled by the Congressional Research Service, with independent analyses from the Center for Biological Diversity estimating the cuts included more than 500 biologists specifically. The Interior Department separately proposed a FY2026 budget cutting the agency's Endangered Species Act listing-activity funding by 36% year-over-year, part of an overall reduction in FWS's average annual appropriations from roughly $1.94 billion (2017-2025 average) to approximately $1.6 billion for FY2026; the same budget proposed eliminating funding for the State and Tribal Wildlife Grant program entirely. The Service also discontinued its Refuge System Strategic Growth Policy and, according to conservation groups, has effectively stopped conducting the annual Florida panther population count last performed in 2015. As of reporting in April 2026, the Fish and Wildlife Service had added zero new species to the federal endangered or threatened species list during Trump's second term, compared to 22 species added during the entirety of his first term, a gap the Center for Biological Diversity described as the longest period without a new listing decision since the Endangered Species Act was implemented. A backlog of more than 400 species remains pending final agency listing decisions.

primary_proponent.name: Doug Burgum
primary_proponent.note: Oversees the Fish and Wildlife Service as part of the Interior Department and directed the FY2026 budget request that cut Endangered Species Act listing funding and proposed eliminating the State and Tribal Wildlife Grant program.
