title: HUD's Fair Housing Office Cut by Roughly Two-Thirds Amid Whistleblower Allegations
institution: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development, Office of Fair Housing and Equal Opportunity
status: Ongoing
date_of_action: 2025-01 through 2025-11 (staffing reductions); enforcement policy changes ongoing through 2026
what_changed: Internal documents revealed in January 2025 that HUD officials, working with the Department of Government Efficiency, planned to cut staff at the Office of Fair Housing and Equal Opportunity (FHEO), which investigates housing discrimination complaints nationwide, by 77%. Following the March 2025 confirmation of Secretary Scott Turner, probationary employees were terminated, according to a former HUD civil rights attorney, 'with no real justification.' By October 2025, Office of Management and Budget data showed 442 total HUD employees had been dismissed since the administration began, including 114 from FHEO specifically, a 45% cut by that count. A subsequent National Fair Housing Alliance analysis published in its November 2025 Fair Housing Trends Report found FHEO then operating with roughly one-third of the staff it had in January 2025, a reduction of nearly two-thirds, reflecting cuts that continued to deepen through an additional 400-plus HUD-wide layoffs issued during the October-November 2025 government shutdown. Outside experts estimated total HUD departures, including voluntary and buyout-driven exits, at as many as 2,300 employees since January 2025. In September 2025, HUD issued a memorandum withdrawing previously issued fair housing enforcement guidance. Days later, four HUD attorneys, including Paul Osadebe, filed a whistleblower complaint with Senator Elizabeth Warren alleging the department was illegally winding down and stalling active fair housing discrimination cases; co-whistleblower Palmer Heenan was fired, and Osadebe was placed on administrative leave before being formally terminated on February 9, 2026.

title: Education Department Cancels $881M in Research Contracts, Halting Major National Data Collections
institution: Institute of Education Sciences (U.S. Department of Education)
status: Complete (contracts terminated in February 2025; no indication of reinstatement as of the last-verified date)
what_changed: On February 10, 2025, the Department of Government Efficiency directed the Department of Education to terminate 89 contracts held by the Institute of Education Sciences (IES), the department's independent research and statistics arm, which DOGE valued at $881 million. The same evening, IES canceled its peer review panels that evaluate the scientific merit of grant applications for its research centers. Canceled work included the National Postsecondary Student Aid Study, a congressionally mandated survey conducted every four years and described by the American Educational Research Association as the 'backbone' of two other longitudinal higher-education studies; the Beginning Postsecondary Students survey; an 11-year study of youths with disabilities that was roughly halfway complete and was meant to serve over 1,000 students in 2025-2026; and U.S. participation in international assessments comparing American student performance with other countries. On February 13, 2025, the Department separately terminated an additional $336-350 million in contracts and grants to Regional Educational Laboratories and Equity Assistance Centers. The Department stated that the National Assessment of Educational Progress, the College Scorecard, and the College Navigator were not affected by the cancellations.

primary_proponent.note: Oversaw the Department of Education as it implemented DOGE's directed contract terminations; the Department issued an official public statement defending the cuts, arguing IES had spent '$14 billion and countless contracts later' without measurable improvement in student outcomes.

estimated_impact.summary: The federal government's primary independent capacity to evaluate its own education programs and collect national statistics on K-12 and higher education students was significantly curtailed in a single week, interrupting data series that researchers say cannot easily resume once broken.

Notably, this is a genuinely mixed-reception story: Mark Schneider, who led IES during Trump's first term, said in a public LinkedIn discussion that the agency needed significant modernization and called the disruption 'an opportunity' rather than a catastrophe, even while acknowledging DOGE had not articulated a modernization plan. Researcher and data organizations, including AERA, the Council of Professional Associations on Federal Statistics, and the Data Quality Campaign, countered that the specific programs cut, especially the National Postsecondary Student Aid Study and international assessment participation, are congressionally mandated, multi-year data series that lose scientific value once interrupted, since gaps cannot be retroactively filled. The Department confirmed core public-facing tools (NAEP, College Scorecard, College Navigator) were not affected, indicating the cuts targeted specific research contracts rather than IES's full range of functions.

title: IMLS Dismantlement Permanently Blocked by Federal Court — A Rare Full Reversal
institution: Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS)
status: Reversed — federal court permanently blocked the dismantlement; agency fully restored via binding DOJ settlement
date_of_action: 2025-03-14 (executive order issued) through 2026-04-09 (binding settlement agreement finalized)
what_changed: On March 14, 2025, Trump issued Executive Order 14238 directing the elimination of IMLS -- the only federal agency dedicated to the nation's libraries and museums. The administration moved quickly: by March 31, all but 12 of the agency's roughly 70-75 employees were placed on administrative leave, with a reduction-in-force notice set for May 4; nearly all IMLS board members were dismissed; and beginning April 1, the agency's Acting Director (Keith Sonderling, who stated he would act 'in lockstep' with the administration) began terminating previously-awarded, congressionally-appropriated grants nationwide, including Grants to States, National Leadership Grants, and Laura Bush 21st Century Librarian grants. Unlike most entries in this tracker, this story does not end in unreversed loss: the American Library Association and AFSCME sued (ALA v. Sonderling), and 21 state attorneys general separately sued in Rhode Island v. Trump. On May 1, 2025, a federal judge issued a temporary restraining order blocking further staff terminations. In November 2025, the U.S. District Court for Rhode Island ruled the dismantlement actions 'arbitrary and capricious and contrary to federal law,' and IMLS reinstated all previously terminated grants that December. On April 6, 2026, the administration withdrew its appeal of that ruling, and on April 9, 2026, ALA, AFSCME, and the Department of Justice signed a binding settlement agreement ensuring IMLS remains intact and continues its congressionally mandated work going forward -- closing the litigation with the agency fully restored, not merely paused.

primary_proponent.name: Keith Sonderling
primary_proponent.role: Acting Director of IMLS (appointed to implement the executive order)
primary_proponent.note: Sonderling stated publicly he would act 'in lockstep' with the administration's directive to eliminate the agency, and personally signed the grant-termination notices sent to states. This is the same official covered in this project's Cabinet-Level Legal Exposure tracker for a related action at the Department of Labor, where a federal court similarly blocked his agency's actions -- a recurring pattern across Sonderling's various acting-director roles during this period.

estimated_impact.summary: ALA President Sam Helmick called the November 2025 court ruling 'a powerful affirmation of what libraries mean to America,' noting it restored 'shared access to books in rural and remote areas, essential virtual learning tools, children's reading programs and the countless library services available to anyone who walks into a public, school or academic library.' During the roughly 8-month period before reinstatement, real, documented harm occurred at the institutional level: the Dubuque Museum of Art in Iowa had already begun hiring and training staff against a $74,979 grant that was frozen: the Science Center of Iowa's planned internship program, backed by a $196,046 grant, was jeopardized. IMLS awarded $266 million in grants to museums and libraries in 2024, the baseline scale of funding that was disrupted before being restored.

Real institutions (documented above) experienced genuine disruption, delayed programming, and staffing uncertainty during the roughly year-long period before restoration, and the Trump administration's FY2027 budget request, released the same day as the settlement, still proposed cutting IMLS funding going forward -- meaning the underlying policy dispute over the agency's funding level remains live even though the dismantlement-without-Congress legal question has been resolved against the administration.

title: IRS Lost 27% of Its Workforce and a Third of Its Auditors — Independent Watchdogs Project $600B in Lost Revenue
institution: Internal Revenue Service (IRS)
status: Ongoing (2026 filing season completed under reduced staff; further cuts proposed for FY2027)
date_of_action: 2025-02 (initial probationary firings) through 2026-04 (most recent GAO/TIGTA reporting)
what_changed: Beginning February 2025, the IRS workforce was cut by approximately 27%, falling from roughly 102,000 employees in January 2025 to about 74,000 by December 2025 -- a reduction of nearly 28,000 people, primarily via probationary-employee terminations (~7,300), deferred-resignation buyouts (~4,700), and further attrition. The cuts were not evenly distributed: Taxpayer Services (which processes returns, answers phones, and handles correspondence) lost about 21-22% of its staff; the agency's IT function lost 27%; and revenue agents -- the specialists who conduct complex audits of high-income individuals, partnerships, and large corporations -- lost roughly 3,600 people, about 31% of the entire auditing workforce. Congress separately cut the IRS enforcement budget by 8% for FY2026 (the lowest inflation-adjusted level since 1988) and cut $1.1 billion (12%) from the IRS's base budget overall, rescinding most of the remaining Inflation Reduction Act funding that had been allocated to rebuild the agency; the base budget is now about 40% below its 2010 level after inflation. The IRS responded by lowering its own phone-service target from 85% to 70% of calls answered, and an independent analysis by the Center for Taxpayer Rights found call wait times on the main line increased more than 70% between February 2025 and February 2026. A March 2026 GAO audit found the IRS's staffing reductions 'were not targeted or strategic' and that the agency still lacks an updated strategic workforce plan. Notably, IRS Commissioner Frank Bisignano simultaneously serves as the Senate-confirmed Commissioner of the Social Security Administration -- see the related SSA entry in this tracker -- splitting his attention across both agencies during this period.

primary_proponent.name: Frank Bisignano
primary_proponent.role: IRS Chief Executive Officer / Commissioner (concurrently SSA Commissioner)
primary_proponent.note: Publicly defended the cuts before the Senate Finance Committee, stating the agency can achieve 'less people and better results,' and that the IRS cut phone-center 'idle time' by nearly 50%. Committee Democrats, led by Ranking Member Ron Wyden, disputed this characterization directly in the same hearing. Bisignano told senators he treats running both SSA and IRS as managing 'two large organizations,' sometimes working 14-hour days across both.

estimated_impact.summary: The Yale Budget Lab modeled the specific 2025 staffing reductions (broken down by IRS operating function, using the agency's own budget-activity categories) and projected the cuts will reduce federal revenue collection by almost $600 billion over the 2026-2035 window -- a striking figure since the IRS collects 96% of all federal revenue, meaning even modest enforcement-capacity losses compound into large fiscal effects. The National Taxpayer Advocate (an independent office within the IRS that reports directly to Congress) has been notably even-handed in its assessments -- praising the 2025 filing season as one of the most successful in recent memory, while explicitly warning that the more severe 2026 season risks are now 'markedly different' given the compounding effect of workforce loss, budget cuts, and simultaneous major tax-law changes from the 2025 reconciliation bill.

title: LIHEAP Heating Assistance Program Staff Eliminated, Funding Delayed for Millions
institution: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP), Administration for Children and Families, HHS
status: Funding eventually released (Nov. 28, 2025); underlying staff elimination not reversed
date_of_action: 2025-04-04 (entire program staff terminated); 2025-10-01 through 2025-11-28 (funding delayed during shutdown)
what_changed: In April 2025, the Trump administration fired the entire federal staff team responsible for administering LIHEAP -- the ~$4.1 billion program that helps roughly 6 million low-income households pay heating and cooling bills, serving all 50 states, D.C., U.S. territories, and federally recognized tribes. This is the same program Trump's budget proposed zero-funding entirely. Six months later, when the October-November 2025 government shutdown (the longest in U.S. history at 43 days) hit, LIHEAP's annual funding allocation -- normally distributed to states by early November -- was delayed with no dedicated federal staff left to expedite the process once Congress acted. States including Pennsylvania, New York, Massachusetts, Minnesota, Connecticut, and Kansas had to delay or suspend their heating-assistance seasons entirely during the onset of winter. The $3.6 billion in delayed funding was finally released to states on November 28, 2025, over two weeks after the shutdown ended (Nov. 12) and roughly a month later than the program's normal early-November distribution.

primary_proponent.name: Trump administration / HHS Administration for Children and Families
primary_proponent.role: Executed the April 2025 staff elimination; oversaw the subsequent funding delay
primary_proponent.note: HHS did not name a specific individual responsible for the staff terminations in available reporting -- flag as agency-level, not individually attributable, though HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. was specifically the recipient of a bipartisan House member letter urging release of the delayed funds. An HHS spokesperson publicly blamed the delay entirely on 'congressional Democrats' without directly answering PBS's question about whether the earlier staff firings affected the agency's ability to process the funding quickly once released.

estimated_impact.summary: Roughly 6 million households nationally depend on LIHEAP, and about 68% of LIHEAP households also receive SNAP -- meaning the same families were simultaneously hit by both the LIHEAP delay and the SNAP funding freeze during the same shutdown window, compounding hardship. An estimated 21 million U.S. households were already behind on energy payments heading into the delay, with nearly 4 million disconnections possible for the season per the Center for Law and Social Policy.

title: MSPB Loses Quorum for 8 Months After Trump Fires Whistleblower Board Chair Cathy Harris
institution: Merit Systems Protection Board
status: Resolved against Harris (the Supreme Court denied her petition for certiorari on June 30, 2026 -- one day after Trump v. Slaughter -- leaving the D.C. Circuit's December 2025 ruling upholding her firing as the final word in her individual case; the Board's quorum was separately restored in October 2025 via an unrelated confirmation, so the practical quorum crisis had already ended, but the removal-power precedent from the D.C. Circuit's 2-1 ruling is now permanent since the Supreme Court declined further review)
date_of_action: 2025-02-10 (firing) through present; the Board's quorum was separately restored in October 2025 via a new member's confirmation, independent of Harris's litigation
what_changed: On February 10, 2025, President Trump fired MSPB Chair Cathy Harris via a one-sentence email without citing any cause, three years before her Senate-confirmed term was set to expire in 2028. Harris sued; a district court granted a temporary restraining order reinstating her on February 18, and on March 4, Judge Rudolph Contreras granted summary judgment finding the firing unlawful. The government appealed, and a three-judge D.C. Circuit panel stayed her reinstatement on March 28. The full en banc D.C. Circuit voted 7-4 to vacate that stay and reinstate Harris on April 7, but two days later, on April 9, Chief Justice Roberts granted the government's emergency application for a stay, again removing Harris and leaving the Board without the quorum needed to issue decisions. In December 2025, a 2-1 D.C. Circuit panel (Judges Katsas and Walker, both Trump appointees) ruled on the merits that Congress cannot restrict the president's authority to remove NLRB or MSPB members who 'wield substantial executive power,' with a dissenting judge warning the majority's reasoning implies 'no independent agencies may lawfully exist in this country.' The Board separately regained a functional quorum in October 2025 through the unrelated Senate confirmation of a new member, James Woodruff. On March 17, 2026, Harris petitioned the Supreme Court for review, arguing her case is legally distinct from the FTC's Slaughter case because MSPB is purely adjudicatory, does not set policy, and structurally resembles multimember bodies with roughly 150 years of established constitutional standing. A group of Senate Democrats filed an amicus brief in April 2026 urging the Court to hear her case. The June 29, 2026 Trump v. Slaughter ruling did not directly resolve Harris's petition but substantially weakens her argument by holding broadly that agencies exercising 'executive power' must have removable leadership. Correction 2026-07-19: the Supreme Court denied Harris's petition for certiorari on June 30, 2026, one day after deciding Trump v. Slaughter -- this entry's prior 'remains formally unresolved' status is now out of date. The denial leaves the D.C. Circuit's December 2025 2-1 panel ruling (Katsas and Walker) as the final word on Harris's individual removal; the Supreme Court's order gave no reasoning beyond the standard denial, consistent with how it typically treats cert denials, so the underlying legal question (whether purely adjudicatory bodies like MSPB warrant different treatment than the FTC) was not resolved on the merits, only left undisturbed as decided below.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Fired Harris directly via a one-sentence email giving no reason, then had the administration argue through multiple rounds of litigation that MSPB members 'wield substantial executive power' and are therefore removable at will, a position a divided D.C. Circuit panel accepted in December 2025.

estimated_impact.summary: MSPB is the sole federal venue for civil servants, including whistleblowers, to appeal illegal firings and retaliation; Harris's removal left the Board without a functioning quorum for roughly eight months in 2025, echoing a prior five-year quorum gap (2017-2022) that left the agency with a backlog of more than 3,500 unresolved cases by the time quorum was restored.

The administration's position, accepted by the D.C. Circuit's December 2025 majority, is that MSPB nonetheless 'wields substantial executive power' and therefore its members must be removable by the president at will. Whistleblower advocates warn the practical stakes are severe because MSPB has exclusive jurisdiction over most federal whistleblower retaliation claims, meaning a quorum loss leaves affected employees with 'no realistic recourse' in the interim. This is a genuinely contested legal question rather than a settled one: a federal district court and the full en banc D.C. Circuit both sided with Harris before a later divided panel reversed course, and her Supreme Court petition remains pending.

title: National Archives Loses Its Archivist and Digital Access Office Amid Deep Budget Cuts
institution: National Archives and Records Administration
status: Ongoing (agency leadership remains unsettled through at least four acting archivists since February 2025; FY2026 budget cuts partially implemented)
date_of_action: 2025-02 (Archivist Colleen Shogan fired) through 2026 (ongoing reorganization, office eliminations, and records-center closures)
what_changed: In February 2025, President Trump fired Archivist of the United States Colleen Shogan without citing cause, an unprecedented removal of the position's confirmed leader. Deputy Archivist William Bosanko briefly became acting archivist before announcing his resignation along with several other senior staff. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was then named acting archivist while continuing his separate Cabinet role, a dual appointment a U.S. senator later suggested may have violated federal law given how long Rubio held both positions simultaneously; Jim Byron subsequently served as senior adviser performing archivist functions before departing in April 2026, when GSA head Edward Forst was named acting archivist. Separately, NARA's FY2026 budget request set the agency's funding at $414.7 million, nearly $60 million less than projected FY2025 spending, eliminating 136 full-time positions agency-wide. The proposal included full elimination of NARA's 50-person Office of Innovation, which runs the agency's online records catalog, digital access initiatives, and the public 'citizen archivist' transcription project, with NARA stating those functions would be 'transferred elsewhere' rather than continued directly. In June 2025, NARA issued reduction-in-force notices affecting roughly 100 employees (about 3% of staff), which two NARA employees said effectively 'wiped out' the Office of Innovation and the division supporting field offices and presidential libraries; the John F. Kennedy Presidential Library was briefly forced to close in February 2025 after probationary employees were terminated. The FY2026 proposal also cut funding for NARA's Electronic Records Initiative by roughly 33%, even as the agency faces a growing volume of incoming digital records, with only about 3% of its roughly 12 billion textual paper pages digitized as of the most recent count. In 2026, NARA also announced it would vacate three federal records centers (Chicago, San Francisco/San Bruno, and Seattle) and sunset its free public History Hub reference forum to save an estimated $370,000 annually.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Personally fired Archivist Colleen Shogan without cause, triggering the leadership churn that has left NARA without a Senate-confirmed archivist for over a year and clearing the way for the subsequent budget and office cuts implemented under a succession of acting leaders.

estimated_impact.summary: The federal agency responsible for preserving and providing public access to government records, including FOIA-releasable historical documents and a growing volume of electronic records from across government, lost its confirmed leadership and saw both its staffing and its main digital-access modernization office cut even as its underlying workload continues to grow.

Critics, including former Archivist Colleen Shogan herself and Jason Baron, a University of Maryland professor and former NARA director of litigation, argue the cuts arrive precisely when NARA faces its greatest resource strain in decades, citing existing FOIA processing backlogs at presidential libraries and the billions of pages of electronic records still arriving from other federal agencies that require both staff and technology to preserve. It is worth noting that reduced public access to government records has been a broader theme researchers and transparency advocates have raised across multiple agencies in this period, though those other agencies' actions are separate from NARA's own decisions described here.

title: National Park Service Lost 24% of Its Permanent Staff — Internal Report Contradicts Interior's Public 'False Narrative' Denial
institution: National Park Service (NPS), Department of the Interior
status: Ongoing (staffing losses not reversed; administration proposing further FY2026 budget cuts)
date_of_action: 2025-02-14 (initial mass layoff, dubbed the 'Valentine's Day Massacre' by affected staff) through 2025-08-27 (internal operational-status report surfaces)
what_changed: On February 14, 2025, NPS laid off approximately 1,000-1,700 probationary employees just weeks before peak visitor season began across the system's 63 national parks and 370 other managed sites. By July 2025, independent analysis of Interior Department workforce data by the National Parks Conservation Association (NPCA) found NPS had lost 24% of its permanent staff overall (about 4,000 people) since January, while only roughly 4,500 of a promised ~8,000 seasonal positions had been filled. In April 2025, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum issued Secretarial Order 3426, mandating that every park remain 'open and accessible' regardless of staffing levels -- meaning parks could not close facilities or reduce services in response to the shortages, only quietly absorb the strain. Per Order 3426's own reporting requirement, every park unit had to submit an updated operational status by July 25, 2025; an internal NPS report compiled from those submissions, first reported by the New York Times, documented widespread, specific impacts: nearly 70 parks reduced visitor center hours, 22 postponed maintenance, and 11 closed or delayed opening facilities. Specific documented examples include all 13 lifeguard positions vacant at Assateague Island National Seashore (leaving the beach unguarded), the fire management team reduced to one person at Cumberland Island National Seashore, and Indiana Dunes National Park's ranger-led tours cut from 575 to approximately 100 for the year after 4 of 6 interpretive-division supervisors took early retirement. When this internal report became public, Interior deputy press secretary Aubrie Spady called the reporting a 'false narrative,' stating 'staffing levels and operations at our National Parks this summer are on par with previous years. Full Stop' -- a direct denial of findings drawn from the agency's own internal, Order-3426-mandated data collection.

primary_proponent.name: Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) / Doug Burgum
primary_proponent.role: DOGE directed the initial layoffs; Interior Secretary Burgum issued the 'stay open regardless of staffing' order that followed
primary_proponent.note: Burgum's Secretarial Order 3426 is the specific policy mechanism that turned a staffing shortage into a documented service-quality crisis -- by prohibiting parks from closing or scaling back in response to short-staffing, the order effectively required remaining staff to absorb the gap rather than allowing an official acknowledgment of reduced capacity. Interior's own deputy press secretary's 'false narrative' denial is difficult to reconcile with the fact that the cited findings came from data Interior itself required parks to report under Burgum's own order.

estimated_impact.summary: NPCA President and CEO Theresa Pierno characterized the situation as predictable: 'This crisis was both predictable and preventable. Gutting staff, freezing seasonal hiring, and pushing out experienced rangers was a recipe for disaster.' Beyond visitor-experience impacts, several documented examples touch public safety and resource protection directly -- the loss of fire response capacity at Cumberland Island, halted pest/disease monitoring (including zoonotic disease surveillance for plague, rabies, and hantavirus) at Grand Canyon, and unstaffed entrance stations at Glacier National Park during normal operating hours.

Congressional Republicans overseeing NPS (Rep. Ryan Zinke, Rep. Tom Tiffany, Sen. Steve Daines) expressed confidence Secretary Burgum could adequately staff parks for the season, a genuinely different assessment from Democratic members and outside advocacy groups, worth presenting as a real point of disagreement among people with access to the same underlying situation, not just a one-sided finding.

title: NCUA Board Reduced to a Single Member After Trump Fires Both Democratic Board Members
institution: National Credit Union Administration
status: Ongoing (Harper and Otsuka filed their D.C. Circuit brief in mid-July 2026 formally arguing NCUA deserves Fed-like removal protection, following the district court's finding that Congress modeled NCUA's structure on the Federal Reserve; no ruling yet, and the two remain off the single-member board as of this update)
date_of_action: 2025-04-15 (firings) through 2026-06-29 (Supreme Court ruling narrowed path to reinstatement)
what_changed: In April 2025, President Trump fired both Democratic members of the National Credit Union Administration's three-person board -- former Chairman Todd Harper and Tanya Otsuka -- by email, without citing cause, leaving Republican Chair Kyle Hauptman as the sole remaining board member. Harper's term was not set to expire until April 2027 and Otsuka's not until August 2029. The Federal Credit Union Act defines the NCUA board as a three-member body with staggered six-year terms, structured (according to a subsequent district court finding) to mirror the Federal Reserve's governance model and insulate credit union regulation from political turnover. With only one board member, the NCUA could continue routine supervision and enforcement but could not vote on new rulemakings or policy changes; a scheduled board meeting was canceled. Harper and Otsuka sued; in July 2025 U.S. District Judge Amir Ali ruled the firings unlawful and ordered reinstatement, finding Congress had deliberately restricted the president's removal power when it added fixed terms to the statute in 1978. The two briefly returned to a board meeting before an appeals court granted the administration an emergency stay, sidelining them again. The case was held in abeyance pending Trump v. Slaughter; when the Supreme Court ruled for the administration on June 29, 2026, credit-union trade groups and legal analysts concluded the ruling 'effectively ends' the Harper/Otsuka challenge, since the Federal Credit Union Act -- unlike the Federal Reserve Act -- contains no explicit 'for cause' removal language for the NCUA to lean on. Harper and Otsuka's attorneys have since asked the D.C. Circuit to let them argue the NCUA deserves Fed-like treatment given its similar market-regulatory structure, but as of this writing they remain off the board, which continues to operate with a single member. Update 2026-07-19: in a filing with the D.C. Circuit in mid-July 2026, Harper and Otsuka's attorneys formally argued NCUA deserves Federal Reserve-like removal protection, leaning on the district court's earlier finding that Congress deliberately modeled NCUA's governance structure on the Fed's -- the same distinction that succeeded for Cook but was rejected for MSPB's Harris. No ruling on this argument had issued as of this update; the appeal remains pending.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Personally ordered the firings of Harper and Otsuka by email in April 2025; the administration's Department of Justice has since argued in filings that the Federal Credit Union Act contains no language restricting presidential removal authority.

estimated_impact.summary: A statutorily three-member, bipartisan financial regulator serving over 140 million credit union members has operated with a single board member for over a year, unable to vote on new rulemakings during that period, with a Supreme Court ruling now narrowing the fired members' path back to the board.

Slaughter's reasoning therefore applies with even less obstacle at the NCUA than it did at the FTC. Harper and Otsuka counter that Congress deliberately modeled the NCUA's structure on the Federal Reserve -- which the same June 29, 2026 ruling explicitly protected -- and that the district court already found this historical intent persuasive. Industry groups (America's Credit Unions, Defense Credit Union Council) have taken a notably neutral, process-focused position, calling mainly for a restored three-person board regardless of who fills the seats, rather than defending either side's substantive position. The litigation over the underlying legal question remains technically open at the D.C. Circuit even though most analysts consider the practical outcome settled.

title: NEA Terminates Over Half of Its Open Grants in a Single Weekend
institution: National Endowment for the Arts
status: Complete for terminated grants (effective May 31, 2025); the agency's proposed full elimination was not enacted as of the last-verified date, though grantmaking priorities remain altered under new leadership
date_of_action: 2025-05-02 to 2025-05-12 (termination notices); leadership confirmed December 19, 2025
what_changed: On the evening of May 2, 2025, hours after the administration released a preliminary FY2026 budget proposing to eliminate the NEA entirely, the agency emailed termination or withdrawal notices to hundreds of grantees nationwide, affecting more than half of its open awards, in amounts ranging from $10,000 to $100,000, effective May 31, 2025. A second round of termination notices followed on May 12, 2025. Affected organizations spanned all 50 states, Puerto Rico, and Washington, D.C., including theaters, orchestras, literary magazines, and community arts programs. Some grantees whose projects appeared to match the NEA's own newly stated priorities were terminated anyway; California Symphony, for example, lost funding for a commission specifically written to celebrate the 250th anniversary of American independence, one of the NEA's own listed new priorities. On May 5, 2025, ten NEA discipline directors and two members of senior leadership announced they would take deferred resignation, departing by the end of May. Mary Anne Carter, who had led the NEA during Trump's first term, was serving as senior adviser overseeing the agency at the time the terminations were issued (the previous Biden-appointed chair, Maria Rosario Jackson, had resigned on Inauguration Day); Carter was confirmed by the Senate as permanent NEA Chair on December 19, 2025. Separately, a federal judge in Rhode Island struck down a related NEA policy disfavoring grant applications the agency deemed to 'promote gender ideology' on September 19, 2025.

primary_proponent.role: Senior Adviser overseeing NEA operations at the time of the terminations; later confirmed as NEA Chair (Dec. 19, 2025)

Critics point to apparent inconsistencies in the rollout, including cases like California Symphony's, where a project matching a stated new priority was terminated anyway, and argue the 7-calendar-day appeal window (which included a weekend) was procedurally unfair given the scale and suddenness of the cuts. It is worth distinguishing this from the separate, better-documented case of the sister agency National Endowment for the Humanities, where a federal court found in a May 2026 summary judgment that a comparable mass grant termination process was unlawful; no equivalent final court ruling specific to these NEA terminations has been identified as of the last-verified date, so that outcome should not be assumed to extend to the NEA.

title: National Endowment for Democracy Funding Withheld, Partially Restored Only After Court Order
institution: National Endowment for Democracy (funded via State Department appropriation)
status: Ongoing (FY2025 funds released under court order; FY2026 funding and DOJ appeal still contested)
date_of_action: 2025-01-22 (funding access first denied) through ongoing litigation as of 2026
what_changed: Since January 22, 2025, the executive branch withheld the National Endowment for Democracy's (NED) access to congressionally appropriated funding, according to NED's federal lawsuit. NED, a nonprofit created by the 1983 National Endowment for Democracy Act, is statutorily directed to receive annual grants through the State Department; it distributes roughly 83% of its funds directly to democracy, free-press, and anti-corruption groups in more than 90 countries. NED sued in March 2025, alleging the withholding of nearly $240 million violated the Administrative Procedure Act, the NED Act, and constitutional separation-of-powers and appropriations provisions. The funding halt forced NED to furlough 75% of its staff and, per the lawsuit, created a 'devastating' cash flow crisis affecting 226 approved grants, 124 board-recommended grants, and 53 'core institute' projects. On August 11, 2025, U.S. District Judge Dabney Friedrich -- a Trump appointee -- granted a preliminary injunction, finding the administration had likely violated the NED Act by conditioning fund disbursement on alignment with 'Administration priorities,' calling that rationale 'not plausible' given repeated delays and shifting justifications. Following the ruling, DOJ released the roughly $95 million that remained withheld from NED's FY2025 appropriation. The administration appealed to the D.C. Circuit on October 9, 2025 (case ongoing as of this writing), and separately proposed eliminating NED's funding entirely in its FY2026 budget request, citing (among other claims) an allegation -- which NED's own fact sheet disputes as false -- that NED funded a Ukrainian group accused of doxing U.S. journalists. As of early 2026 court filings, the parties remained in disputes over whether and when NED's FY2026 appropriation would be obligated and disbursed.

primary_proponent.name: Russell Vought
primary_proponent.role: Director, Office of Management and Budget
primary_proponent.note: Named as a defendant in NED's lawsuit; OMB was identified in court filings as the office directing that NED's funding be held for 'review for alignment' with administration priorities, the practice the district court found unlawful.

estimated_impact.summary: A congressionally mandated, four-decade-old democracy-promotion grantmaker had roughly $240 million in appropriated funding withheld outside the normal appropriations process, forcing mass staff furloughs and program suspensions, with only partial and litigation-dependent restoration to date.

Separately, the administration and allied think tanks (e.g., the Center for Renewing America) have longstanding, previously stated ideological objections to NED's mission, characterizing it as a 'partisan political weapon' -- a position predating this specific funding dispute. The court's ruling was narrowly about the legality of withholding already-appropriated funds outside statutory process, not a ruling on whether Congress should fund NED going forward, which remains a live and separate political question in the FY2026 appropriations process. This entry is included partly for balance: it is a case where a court, including a Trump-appointed judge, ordered relief and the administration partially complied -- though the underlying funding fight continues on appeal and into FY2026.

title: National Endowment for the Humanities Grants Terminated, Staff Cut, Funds Redirected
institution: National Endowment for the Humanities (NEH)
status: Largely Complete (staff cuts and grant terminations executed; council dissolved)
date_of_action: 2025-04-02 (initial grant terminations) through 2025-10-01 (National Council on the Humanities firings)
what_changed: Beginning April 2025, NEH terminated more than 1,000 previously-awarded, congressionally-funded grants covering fiscal years 2021-2025, informing 56 state and jurisdiction humanities councils their funding was being 'repurposed in a new direction in furtherance of President Trump's agenda.' NEH then placed the majority of its staff on immediate administrative leave, followed by formal termination notices to 65% of its ~180 employees (about 117 people) in April 2025 -- letters described the cuts as necessary to comply with a Trump executive order directing DOGE to eliminate 'waste, bloat, and insularity.' NEH Chair Shelly C. Lowe, the first Native American to head the agency, was forced out about a month before the mass layoffs. In October 2025, during the government shutdown, all but 4 of the 26 members of the National Council on the Humanities (the body that advises NEH on funding priorities) were fired by email from the White House Office of Presidential Personnel. Separately, the 2025 tax-and-spending law directed NEH to contribute $40 million toward constructing Trump's proposed 'National Garden of American Heroes' sculpture garden -- redirecting endowment funds toward a specific administration priority rather than the competitive peer-reviewed grants the agency traditionally funds.

No single named individual was identified as personally directing the NEH-specific cuts beyond this White House personnel office role -- flag as a diffuse, White-House-directed action rather than one attributable official.

title: NIH Grant Freeze Found to Violate the Impoundment Control Act (GAO)
institution: National Institutes of Health (NIH), Department of Health and Human Services
status: GAO Legal Finding Issued (nonbinding; underlying funding dispute partially reversed, full compliance unconfirmed)
date_of_action: 2026-08-05 (GAO report issued); underlying actions Feb-July 2025
what_changed: Following Trump executive orders in early 2025 targeting DEI and 'gender ideology' funding, NIH terminated more than 1,800 previously-awarded research grants and, separately, obligated roughly $8 billion less in new/continuing grant funding between February and June 2025 than in the same period the prior year -- a drop of more than a third. NIH also paused grant review meetings entirely from late January to early March 2025, delaying funds to hospitals and universities. The Government Accountability Office (GAO), a nonpartisan congressional watchdog, concluded on August 5, 2025 that these actions violated the Impoundment Control Act of 1974, which requires the executive branch to spend congressionally appropriated funds unless it follows a specific legal process to formally request a rescission -- a process NIH/HHS did not use. GAO noted HHS had taken down a public spending-tracking website, which itself hampered GAO's ability to investigate.

primary_proponent.name: Russell Vought
primary_proponent.role: Director, Office of Management and Budget
primary_proponent.note: Named directly by House Appropriations ranking member Rosa DeLauro as responsible alongside Trump: 'GAO has determined that President Trump and OMB Director Vought illegally withheld billions in funding.' This is the same official and legal mechanism (Impoundment Control Act) already tracked in the Cabinet-Level Legal Exposure database's Vought entry -- that entry covers a parallel GAO finding regarding OMB more broadly; this entry is specific to the NIH funding impoundment.

estimated_impact.summary: GAO's own finding is that $8 billion in already-appropriated NIH research funding was illegally withheld over a 5-month period -- money intended for research into cancer, Alzheimer's, ALS, diabetes, mental health, and maternal mortality, per Senate Appropriations Vice Chair Patty Murray's public statement. This was the fifth GAO impoundment finding against the administration in recent weeks as of August 2025, alongside similar findings on EV charging infrastructure funding, museum/library funding, Head Start funding, and K-12 energy efficiency funding -- suggesting a pattern rather than an isolated incident.

A federal court separately ruled in June 2025 that the underlying grant cancellations were illegal in a different proceeding. HHS's public response pointed to a partial resumption of grant review meetings (837 held between March 24-June 30, 2025, more than the same period the prior year) as evidence of good-faith correction, but GAO found this did not address the core funding-obligation shortfall, and could not confirm whether a subsequent July 2025 OMB-directed pause on NIH grants/contracts/training had been fully reversed.

title: NIST workforce reduction and standards/research budget cuts
institution: National Institute of Standards and Technology (Department of Commerce)
status: Ongoing (staff reductions already executed; a further Reduction-in-Force and reorganization plan pending as of early 2026; FY2026 budget still being negotiated in Congress)
date_of_action: 2025-02 to 2026-01 (workforce cuts); FY2026 budget proposal released June 2025
what_changed: NIST, the 125-year-old nonregulatory agency that sets the country's measurement, cybersecurity, and technology standards (including the widely used Cybersecurity Framework, the AI Risk Management Framework, and post-quantum encryption standards), lost more than 700 positions between the start of the Trump administration and January 2026 through resignations, retirements, and voluntary deferments, including roughly 500 recent hires who were let go early in 2025 while still in probationary status. A lab responsible for testing and validating the federal government's encryption implementations lost 89 staff. The administration's FY2026 budget request proposed cutting NIST's $1.2 billion budget by $325 million, eliminating the Hollings Manufacturing Extension Partnership Program entirely (97 positions, $175 million, serving small and mid-size manufacturers in all 50 states and Puerto Rico) and cutting $125 million and 556 positions from NIST's core Scientific and Technical Research and Services program, citing cybersecurity/privacy, health and biological measurements, and physical-infrastructure research as target areas. House appropriators later rejected the steepest of these cuts and restored funding above the administration's request, but the agency has already absorbed the workforce losses and, as of early 2026, is still awaiting a formal Reduction-in-Force and reorganization plan.

primary_proponent.name: Howard Lutnick
primary_proponent.role: U.S. Secretary of Commerce, whose department oversees NIST and sought roughly a 20% department-wide staff cut without using formal layoffs
primary_proponent.note: A bipartisan congressional letter to Lutnick in April 2025 (from the Maryland and Colorado delegations, home to major NIST facilities) demanded details on the reduction-in-force plan, citing the department's own reported goal of cutting 20% of Commerce staff without formal layoffs and warning of 'irreparable' damage to NIST's ability to recruit and retain scientific talent.

estimated_impact.summary: A sustained loss of specialized technical staff and a proposed further budget cut have weakened NIST's capacity in encryption/post-quantum cryptography, AI safety standards, and manufacturing extension services relied on by industry and other federal agencies.

title: NLRB Loses Statutory Quorum for Nearly a Year After Trump Fires Board Member Wilcox
institution: National Labor Relations Board
status: Complete (Wilcox's removal is now considered constitutionally settled following Trump v. Slaughter, June 29, 2026, though the Supreme Court has not yet formally disposed of her specific case)
date_of_action: 2025-01-27 (firing) through 2026-06-29 (Supreme Court's Slaughter ruling resolves the underlying legal question); board lacked a working quorum for roughly 11 of the intervening months
what_changed: On January 27, 2025, President Trump fired NLRB Board Member Gwynne Wilcox without citing any of the statutory grounds (neglect of duty or malfeasance) required by the National Labor Relations Act, leaving the five-seat Board with only two active members, below the three-member quorum required to issue decisions. Wilcox sued on February 5, 2025. U.S. District Judge Beryl Howell ruled the firing unlawful on March 6, 2025, calling it a 'blatant violation of the law' and a 'power grab,' and ordered Wilcox reinstated, which briefly restored the Board's quorum. A three-judge D.C. Circuit panel stayed that ruling on March 28; the full en banc D.C. Circuit reversed course and reinstated Wilcox again on April 7; Chief Justice Roberts then issued an emergency stay removing her again on April 9. On May 22, 2025, the Supreme Court declined to reinstate Wilcox in a 6-3 emergency-docket order, explicitly not a ruling on the merits. The Board remained below quorum until regaining one in December 2025 through new Trump nominations. On January 28, 2026, the D.C. Circuit declined en banc review of the panel decision upholding Wilcox's termination. On June 29, 2026, the Supreme Court's decision in the related case Trump v. Slaughter overruled the 90-year-old Humphrey's Executor precedent underlying Wilcox's legal theory; legal analysts across firms now consider her removal constitutionally settled even though the Court has not yet formally disposed of her specific still-pending case.

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Fired Wilcox directly via a short email citing loss of confidence in her leadership, then pursued the legal theory that his Article II authority permitted the removal all the way to the Supreme Court, a position the Court substantially validated in the related Slaughter case.

estimated_impact.summary: The National Labor Relations Board, the sole federal agency responsible for enforcing private-sector labor law and adjudicating unfair-labor-practice and union-election disputes, was left without a functioning quorum for nearly all of 2025, halting its ability to issue binding decisions during that period.

estimated_impact.caveat: The administration's position, ultimately validated in substance by the Supreme Court's Slaughter ruling, is that the President's Article II authority requires the ability to remove officials exercising executive power at will, regardless of statutory for-cause protections. Labor advocates and a 20-state coalition of attorneys general (led by California AG Bonta, in an amicus brief) argued the firing created a 'dangerous regulatory vacuum' that left workers without a functioning forum to resolve unfair-labor-practice complaints or conduct union elections for most of a year. Notably, the legal question was genuinely unsettled for over a year: a federal district court and an initial D.C. Circuit panel sided with Wilcox, before the en banc D.C. Circuit, a Supreme Court emergency stay, and ultimately the Slaughter precedent sided with the administration, reflecting real legal uncertainty rather than a one-sided dispute.

Circuit's en banc and panel rulings, the Supreme Court's emergency stay and later Slaughter opinion), a California Department of Justice press release quoting the district court's language, and extensive corroborating litigation tracking from four independent labor-and-employment law firms.

title: NOAA Climate Research Zeroed Out in FY2026 Budget Proposal, Princeton Partnership Defunded
institution: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration
status: Partially implemented (the Princeton funding cut took effect; the broader FY2026 budget eliminating NOAA's climate research office was rejected by key congressional spending panels, though the administration has proceeded with related contract terminations regardless)
date_of_action: 2025-04-08 (Princeton funding cut announced) through the FY2026 budget cycle (proposal released July 2025; Congress had not enacted it as of the last-verified date)
what_changed: On April 8, 2025, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick announced the termination of nearly $4 million in NOAA cooperative research funding to Princeton University, effective June 30, 2025, covering three programs including the Cooperative Institute for Modeling the Earth System, which supports Princeton's partnership with NOAA's Geophysical Fluid Dynamics Laboratory (GFDL), widely regarded as producing some of the world's most advanced climate modeling. The Commerce Department's official announcement stated the program 'promotes exaggerated and implausible climate threats, contributing to a phenomenon known as climate anxiety.' In July 2025, NOAA's proposed FY2026 budget was reported to contain a literal line reading 'Total, Climate Research: $0,' along with the proposed elimination of the entire Office of Oceanic and Atmospheric Research (OAR), NOAA's research arm, and defunding of all of OAR's climate and weather cooperative institutes nationwide, including CIRES at the University of Colorado Boulder, which houses the National Snow and Ice Data Center. The overall proposal called for cutting NOAA's budget by more than 25% and its ~13,000-person workforce by roughly 17%, along with a 44% reduction in planned investment in next-generation geostationary weather satellites. Congressional spending panels rejected the FY2026 budget request as proposed, but the administration proceeded with some of the changes administratively regardless, including terminating contracts for planned satellite instruments in August 2025 as what agency officials described to staff as a 'down payment' on the broader proposed cuts.

primary_proponent.name: Howard Lutnick
primary_proponent.note: Personally announced and was quoted defending the Princeton funding termination, and oversees the Commerce Department's NOAA budget proposals implementing the broader climate-research defunding.

estimated_impact.summary: A federal agency responsible for the data and modeling underlying weather forecasting, hurricane prediction, and climate research had its dedicated climate research funding line proposed at zero and lost a 57-year research partnership with one of the world's leading climate modeling laboratories.

title: NRC Commissioner Fired Without Cause, Agency Independence and Staffing Cut Under DOGE Reorganization
institution: Nuclear Regulatory Commission
status: Ongoing (firing final and unlitigated; DOGE-directed reorganization and regulatory rewrite still in progress)
date_of_action: 2025-05-23 (executive order) and 2025-06-13 (Hanson firing)
what_changed: On June 13, 2025, President Trump fired NRC Commissioner Christopher T. Hanson, effective immediately, via a two-line email from the White House Presidential Personnel Office. It was the first removal of an NRC commissioner since the agency's creation in 1974; Hanson's term was not set to expire until 2029, and no cause was cited beyond a White House spokesperson's comment that 'all organizations are more effective when leaders are rowing in the same direction.' Hanson, originally appointed by Trump in his first term and later chaired the commission under Biden, said he was terminated 'without cause, contrary to existing law and longstanding precedent regarding removal of independent agency appointees.' The firing left the five-member commission at a 2-2 partisan split with one vacancy, and came three weeks after Trump signed Executive Order 14300, 'Ordering the Reform of the Nuclear Regulatory Commission' (May 23, 2025), which directs the NRC to reorganize and undertake reductions in force in consultation with its DOGE Team, shrink the Advisory Committee on Reactor Safeguards to the statutory minimum, and complete a 'wholesale revision' of its safety regulations and guidance documents within 18 months, with rulemaking routed through OMB Director Russell Vought's office -- a process not previously applied to this traditionally arms-length safety regulator. Career staff departures followed; NRC's No. 2 career operations official, a 32-year veteran and self-described two-time Trump voter, retired in May 2025 partly in protest of political operatives being embedded in senior leadership positions with what he called potential to 'significantly influence decision-making.'

primary_proponent.name: Donald Trump
primary_proponent.note: Personally ordered Hanson's termination via the White House Presidential Personnel Office and signed EO 14300 directing DOGE-guided NRC workforce reductions and a wholesale regulatory rewrite routed through OMB Director Russell Vought.

title: DOGE Terminated 1,752 NSF Research Grants Using a Senator's Keyword List, Then NSF Formally Weakened Its Own Peer Review Standards
institution: National Science Foundation (NSF)
status: Largely complete (terminations executed; peer-review rule changes formally in effect since Dec. 15, 2025)
date_of_action: 2025-04-14 (DOGE arrives at NSF) through 2025-12-15 (formal peer-review process change takes effect)
what_changed: On April 14, 2025, three DOGE personnel arrived at NSF headquarters and were given direct access to the agency's internal grant management systems -- an unusual level of access for political appointees into a agency's live award-processing systems, which one NSF program officer told Nature 'raises the hairs on the back of our neck.' DOGE ordered NSF's grants division to pull back all proposals that had already cleared full peer review and internal approval but were awaiting final signoff, instructing career program officers to perform undefined 'mitigation work' to bring the proposals into compliance with Trump executive orders restricting research related to diversity, equity, and inclusion or climate change. NSF ultimately terminated 1,752 active grants worth $1.4 billion. The screening process relied substantially on a list of roughly 3,400 grants compiled by Sen. Ted Cruz's office, which Cruz characterized as funding 'neo-Marxist class warfare propaganda'; independent analysis by the Grant Watch project found the terminated grants' titles and abstracts most frequently contained the words 'Black,' 'climate,' 'student,' 'justice,' 'identity,' and 'teacher' -- and that NSF's STEM Education directorate alone lost 839 grants worth $888 million, nearly half of all dollars terminated agency-wide despite being a small fraction of NSF's total budget. NSF Director Sethuraman Panchanathan resigned during this period. Separately, effective December 15, 2025, NSF formally revised its own peer review standards (PAPPG 24-1 Supplement 1): the minimum number of required external reviews per proposal dropped from three to two, one of which may now be an NSF staff member rather than an outside expert; panel discussions became optional rather than required; and panel summary write-ups were capped at three to five sentences, down from what had often run several paragraphs. NSF attributed the peer-review changes to a proposal backlog and workforce reductions, not to the DOGE-driven terminations directly.

primary_proponent.name: DOGE personnel Luke Farritor and Zachary Terrell
primary_proponent.role: DOGE staff embedded at NSF with direct grant-system access
primary_proponent.note: Then-NSF Director Sethuraman Panchanathan wrote in an internal priorities update that efforts to broaden research participation 'should not preference some groups at the expense of others' -- the stated policy rationale for the review -- before resigning shortly afterward. Sen. Ted Cruz is the named source of the keyword-flagged grant list that substantially shaped which proposals were targeted, though NSF and the White House have not published an official list or criteria of their own, leaving outside researchers (Grant Watch) to reconstruct the pattern independently from grant titles and abstracts.

estimated_impact.summary: Congress separately and explicitly rejected a much larger cut: the FY2026 appropriations bill (signed January 23, 2026) funded NSF at $8.75 billion, refusing the White House's request to cut the agency to $3.9 billion (roughly 55%), which NSF's own internal projections said would have dropped grant funding rates to approximately 7%. The Senate Commerce Committee described the enacted funding level as preventing 'an existential threat to U.S. science funding.' This creates a notable split: Congress protected NSF's overall budget, while DOGE's targeted, keyword-driven terminations and the subsequent peer-review rule changes altered the agency's internal operations and standards in ways that did not require, and were not subject to, the same congressional appropriations fight.

title: National Weather Service Lost ~600 Staff, Including a Vacant Position Tied to the Texas Flood Warnings
institution: National Weather Service (NWS), National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), Department of Commerce
status: Partially reversed (active rehiring underway, but experience/institutional-knowledge loss is not recoverable by rehiring alone)
date_of_action: Early-mid 2025 (initial cuts); ongoing reorganization through at least April 2026
what_changed: Early in 2025, DOGE-driven early retirement incentives and probationary-employee firings cut roughly 600 employees (about 15% of staff) from the National Weather Service nationwide, part of broader cuts to its parent agency NOAA. By May 2025, 52 of the NWS's 122 weather forecast offices had staffing vacancy rates above 20%, and at least 8 offices could no longer maintain 24/7 operations, including Sacramento, Goodland (KS), and Jackson (KY). Former NWS directors wrote publicly that 'our worst nightmare is that weather forecast offices will be so understaffed that there will be needless loss of life.' Specifically at the Austin/San Antonio forecast office -- which covers the Texas Hill Country -- the warning coordination meteorologist position (responsible for working directly with emergency managers and the public on what to do when a disaster strikes) had been vacant since the person who held it for decades accepted an early-retirement offer at the end of April 2025, roughly two months before the deadly July 4, 2025 Guadalupe River flash flood that killed more than 100 people. Two days after that flood, the administration issued a memo stating no federal vacancies could be filled until October, after the extreme weather season ended. In 2026, NOAA began actively rehiring (recruiting ~150 entry-level meteorologists, filling 200+ positions since late 2025), but experienced meteorologists warn the resulting workforce skews inexperienced -- 'because there are no old-timers left,' as one Alaska-based former NWS meteorologist put it -- and 'upper air' atmospheric data collection has noticeably degraded since January 2025, a data source considered fundamental to forecast accuracy.

primary_proponent.name: DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) / NOAA leadership
primary_proponent.note: No single named individual was identified in available reporting as personally directing the NWS-specific cuts -- this was part of the broader, DOGE-driven federal workforce reduction effort. NOAA Administrator Neil Jacobs stated during his confirmation hearing that he would 'ensure that staffing the Weather Service offices is a top priority,' an on-the-record commitment worth checking against subsequent NWS staffing trends.

estimated_impact.summary: The Center for American Progress's analysis of the Texas floods specifically is careful to note the Austin/San Antonio office still issued timely, accurate, impacts-based forecasts despite the vacancy -- crediting 'the extraordinary dedication of the current staff' -- while also stating plainly that the vacant warning coordination meteorologist role is 'responsible for working with emergency managers and the public to ensure people know what to do when a disaster strikes,' the exact communication function that came under scrutiny after the floods. This is a case where the forecasting itself held up, but a specific staffing gap affected the human coordination layer between forecast and public action.

The claim that the Texas floods' death toll was directly caused by the staffing vacancy is contested and not established as fact -- CAP's own analysis credits the Austin/San Antonio office with accurate, timely forecasts despite the vacancy; the more defensible claim is that a specific, relevant coordination role was vacant at the time, not that its vacancy caused specific deaths.

confidence_note: Well-documented from multiple independent, non-advocacy sources (CBS News, NBC News, Center for American Progress with named individuals on the record) plus a direct union vacancy tally. The Texas-flood-specific vacancy is a verified fact (confirmed timing of the retirement, confirmed role description) -- the caveat above about causation should be maintained carefully in any debate use, since overclaiming direct causation would be both inaccurate and vulnerable to easy rebuttal given CAP's own even-handed framing.

title: Peace Corps Headquarters Staff Cut by Roughly 40%
institution: Peace Corps
status: Ongoing (governmentwide RIF moratorium expired Feb. 13, 2026; agency proceeding toward a 575-FTE headquarters target)
date_of_action: 2025-03 to present
what_changed: Beginning in March 2025, DOGE staff directed Peace Corps supervisors to compile lists of personnel and programs to cut, following the earlier governmentwide 'Fork in the Road' deferred resignation offer. By May 2025, roughly 250 employees took a second deferred-resignation push after being told they faced termination if they declined. In September 2025, the Peace Corps Union received formal notice of intent to involuntarily separate 26 bargaining-unit and 11 non-bargaining-unit employees, part of a plan to bring headquarters down to 575 full-time-equivalent staff. That RIF notice period was tolled during the Oct. 1-Nov. 12, 2025 government shutdown and then again by a continuing-resolution moratorium on RIFs through Jan. 30, 2026. The governmentwide RIF moratorium expired Feb. 13, 2026, clearing the way for the cuts to proceed. Recruitment and placement staff have been hit especially hard, with reporting describing a roughly two-thirds reduction in the office responsible for outreach, application review, and volunteer placement across more than 60 countries. The cuts are paired with an agency goal to more than double the number of active volunteers, from about 3,000 to 8,000 by 2030.

primary_proponent.name: Richard E. Swarttz
primary_proponent.role: Acting Director, Peace Corps (Chief of Staff since Sept. 2025; led the Trump-Vance Agency Review Team for Peace Corps during the presidential transition)
primary_proponent.note: Swarttz led the transition-period review team that first identified the agency for downsizing, then oversaw implementation as Chief of Staff before being named Acting Director on Jan. 30, 2026, the position from which he was formally asked by 21 members of Congress on Feb. 19, 2026 to justify the ongoing reductions before proceeding further.

estimated_impact.summary: Peace Corps headquarters has lost close to 40% of its personnel since the start of the administration, with recruitment and placement functions cut especially deep even as the agency has set a goal to more than double its volunteer corps by 2030.

The agency disputes the framing that cuts are proceeding without adequate review: Peace Corps officials told Government Executive in February 2026 that they had already completed a review of the agency's operational structure before moving forward, and the agency has publicly committed to using expanded AI-assisted screening and predictive analytics to help recruiters do more with fewer staff. Peace Corps also states it has no plans to close additional country posts specifically due to staffing (as distinct from a small number of posts closed for unrelated security reasons, e.g. Mozambique in 2025). Whether the RIF fully proceeded after the Feb. 13, 2026 moratorium expiration, or whether Congress's Feb. 19 request for a pause was granted, is not yet confirmed in public reporting as of this entry's last-verified date.

The primary remaining uncertainty is the RIF's final implementation status after Feb. 13, 2026, since no public reporting as of the last-verified date confirms completion.

title: Small Business Administration Cuts 43% of Staff
institution: Small Business Administration
status: Complete (reorganization announced and implemented in 2025); a related plan to hand SBA the federal student loan portfolio was separately blocked in court and abandoned
date_of_action: 2025-03-21 (announced); implemented through 2025
what_changed: On March 21, 2025, the SBA announced it would eliminate approximately 2,700 active positions out of a workforce of nearly 6,500, a 43% reduction, through voluntary resignations, the expiration of COVID-era and other term appointments, and a limited number of formal reductions in force. The agency described the goal as returning to staffing levels from the first Trump administration. The same day, President Trump separately announced that the SBA would immediately take over the Education Department's roughly $1.6-1.7 trillion federal student loan portfolio, a move announced on the same day as, and in apparent tension with, an executive order to begin dismantling the Department of Education. A federal judge blocked the loan-portfolio transfer in May 2025 on the grounds the SBA lacked statutory authority to administer student loans under the Higher Education Act. The administration ultimately abandoned the SBA option and, in March 2026, signed an interagency agreement to move the loan portfolio to the Treasury Department instead.

primary_proponent.name: Kelly Loeffler
primary_proponent.role: SBA Administrator (confirmed by the Senate in February 2025; former U.S. Senator from Georgia)
primary_proponent.note: Loeffler announced and publicly defended the cuts in a video statement, saying the SBA had 'doubled its workforce' since the pandemic with 'miserable results' and that it was time to 'rightsize the agency.'

estimated_impact.summary: The SBA lost close to half its workforce in a single reorganization, one of the larger proportional staff cuts of any agency in this tracker, while briefly being slated to also absorb a student loan portfolio more than 100 times larger than its existing loan book before that plan was blocked and abandoned.

Critics, including the advocacy group Small Business Majority, dispute this characterization, arguing the agency was already understaffed relative to demand and that the cuts create real risk to service delivery, particularly given a separate federal-funds freeze affecting the $5 billion State Small Business Credit Initiative around the same period. Separately, the plan to route federal student loans through the SBA never took effect: it was blocked by a federal court in May 2025 on statutory-authority grounds and abandoned in March 2026 in favor of a Treasury Department transfer, so the staffing cuts at SBA were not, in the end, paired with the added workload critics had warned the reduced agency could not handle.

confidence_note: Strong. The core staffing figures come directly from the SBA's own official press release, corroborated independently by CBS News and PBS NewsHour on the same day, with a converging headcount figure (~3,800 post-reorg) from a Center for American Progress policy analysis. The student-loan-transfer subplot is well corroborated across BestColleges, SoFi, NPR, and a Goldey-Beacom College summary tracking the transfer's eventual redirection to Treasury.

title: White House-directed content review and exhibit alterations at the Smithsonian
institution: Smithsonian Institution (Washington, DC museums, via White House Domestic Policy Council/OMB)
status: Ongoing (review targeting first-phase museums largely complete; White House published findings report July 2026; second-phase museums and further 'content corrections' still pending)
date_of_action: 2025-03-27 (executive order) through 2026-07 (findings report)
what_changed: President Trump's March 27, 2025 executive order 'Restoring Truth and Sanity to American History' directed Vice President JD Vance, who sits on the Smithsonian's Board of Regents, to work to 'remove improper ideology' from Smithsonian museums, and instructed OMB and the Vice President to condition future appropriations on exhibits not 'degrading shared American values' or 'dividing Americans based on race.' In August 2025, White House aide Lindsey Halligan, Domestic Policy Council Director Vince Haley, and OMB Director Russell Vought sent a letter to Smithsonian Secretary Lonnie Bunch launching a formal review of eight flagship DC museums (including the National Museum of American History, the National Museum of African American History and Culture, and the National Portrait Gallery), demanding exhibition texts, future exhibition plans, internal curatorial guidelines, staff manuals, and organizational charts, with a 120-day deadline for museums to begin 'content corrections' replacing 'divisive or ideologically driven language.' The National Museum of American History removed references to Trump's two first-term impeachments from a display in July 2025 as part of an internal content review the Smithsonian undertook under White House pressure; the references were restored about a week later after public criticism. The Smithsonian's Office of Diversity was closed. National Portrait Gallery director Kim Sajet resigned in mid-2025 after Trump publicly said he was firing her, calling her 'a highly partisan person, and a strong supporter of DEI' (the Smithsonian said only Secretary Bunch could make that personnel decision). Artist Amy Sherald canceled a planned exhibition of her work at the National Portrait Gallery in late July 2025 following a dispute over a painting depicting a transgender Statue-of-Liberty figure. In July 2026, the White House Domestic Policy Council published a report titled 'Saving America's Story' accusing the National Museum of American History of 'extreme political activism' and faulting it for not having created a dedicated exhibit on the Founding Fathers, the Declaration of Independence, or the Revolutionary War even in the 250th-anniversary year.

primary_proponent.name: Lindsey Halligan
primary_proponent.role: Special Assistant to the President and Senior Associate Staff Secretary, White House
primary_proponent.note: Co-signed and helped lead the August 2025 letter to Secretary Bunch launching the formal Smithsonian museum review, and, per legal analysis, has 'sought to influence the Smithsonian's decisions, including by instigating a curatorial review of the museum,' despite being a non-Senate-confirmed executive branch appointee with no formal authority over the historically independent institution.

estimated_impact.summary: A White House-directed content review, backed by the implicit threat of withholding congressionally appropriated funds, has already produced at least one exhibit alteration, contributed to the departure of two museum leaders, and led to the cancellation of at least one planned exhibition, at an institution that hosted nearly 17 million visitors in the prior year.

estimated_impact.caveat: The White House frames this as restoring historical accuracy and 'American exceptionalism' rather than suppressing content, and its July 2026 report argues specific exhibits (e.g., 'The Shape of Power,' framing on 'White culture' at NMAAHC) promote an ideological rather than factual narrative. The Smithsonian has consistently maintained its independence, stating its content is grounded in 'scholarly excellence, rigorous research, and the accurate, factual presentation of history,' has said it was not directed by the administration to remove the impeachment references (calling that specific placard 'temporary' and not meeting museum standards on its own), and Secretary Bunch has publicly reaffirmed the institution's independence from political influence in a September 2025 letter to staff. The Smithsonian's unique legal status as a congressionally chartered 'trust instrumentality' - not a standard executive-branch agency - is itself a live, unresolved legal question about whether the White House has authority to compel these changes at all.

The characterization of specific exhibit content and the White House's July 2026 findings report reflect the administration's own framing and are presented here as such, not verified as fact.

title: SNAP Benefits Withheld During 2025 Government Shutdown
institution: U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), Food and Nutrition Service
status: Resolved (shutdown ended, full funding restored through Sept. 2026) but litigation exposed underlying legal violations
date_of_action: 2025-10-31 through 2025-11-13 (peak crisis); shutdown began Oct. 2025, was the longest in U.S. history
what_changed: During the government shutdown, the Trump administration initially announced SNAP benefits (food stamps for ~42 million Americans, about 1 in 8 people in the U.S.) would not be paid in November 2025 at all, breaking from the precedent of prior administrations tapping a contingency fund to keep benefits flowing during shutdowns. After lawsuits, a federal judge in Rhode Island (John McConnell) first ordered partial funding (~65% of normal benefits) from the contingency fund, then found the administration's partial-payment approach itself 'arbitrary and capricious' and ordered full funding by a set deadline. Some Democratic-led states issued full benefits before the Supreme Court intervened with a temporary stay blocking the order, creating a chaotic situation where some recipients got full benefits and others got partial or none, based on which state they lived in and how quickly that state's officials acted. USDA then ordered states to 'immediately undo' full benefit issuances already made. The dispute was ultimately mooted when Congress passed a bill ending the shutdown with SNAP funded through September 2026.

primary_proponent.name: Trump administration / USDA leadership
primary_proponent.role: Executive branch decision not to use the SNAP contingency fund
primary_proponent.note: Attorney General Pam Bondi publicly defended the administration's position, calling Judge McConnell's order 'judicial activism at its worst' and arguing a 'single district court in Rhode Island should not be able to seize center stage.' USDA official Patrick Penn signed the memo directing states to reverse full benefit payments already issued. Judge McConnell's own order found the administration's justification for partial-only payment was 'arbitrary and capricious' and suggested the delay may have been for 'political reasons.'

estimated_impact.summary: Nearly 42 million Americans -- disproportionately children, seniors, and people with disabilities -- had food assistance disrupted or fully withheld for a period during the shutdown, forcing food banks nationwide into 'unprecedented demand' per multiple contemporaneous reports, and leaving benefit amounts dependent on which state a recipient lived in rather than a uniform national standard.

The 'arbitrary and capricious' finding is Judge McConnell's, a single district court, not an appellate or Supreme Court determination.

The judicial 'arbitrary and capricious' finding and named-official statements (Bondi, Penn, multiple governors) make this well-documented and attributable, though the underlying legal question was never fully resolved on the merits -- frame as 'the administration's conduct during this episode was found arbitrary and capricious by one federal judge,' not as a final, appellate-level legal determination.
